The **Royal Navy** (**RN**) is the [naval warfare](/wiki/Naval_warfare "Naval warfare") force of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), responsible for defending the UK, the [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies"), and the [Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories") from naval attack or invasion. It is a component of [His Majesty's Naval Service](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Naval_Service "His Majesty's Naval Service"), and its officers hold their commissions from the [King](/wiki/King_of_the_United_Kingdom "King of the United Kingdom"). Although warships were used by [English](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and [Scottish](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") kings from the early [medieval period](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), the first major maritime engagements were fought in the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War") against [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France"). The modern Royal Navy traces its origins to the English Navy of the early 16th century; the oldest of the [UK's armed services](/wiki/British_Armed_Forces "British Armed Forces"), it is consequently known as the **Senior Service**.

From the early 18th century until the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), it was the world's most powerful navy.[[4]](#cite_note-rodgercommand-8) The Royal Navy played a key part in establishing and defending the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), and four [Imperial fortress](/wiki/Imperial_fortress "Imperial fortress") colonies and a string of imperial bases and coaling stations secured the Royal Navy's ability to assert naval superiority. Following [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), it was significantly reduced in size.[[5]](#cite_note-9) During the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), the Royal Navy transformed into a primarily [anti-submarine force](/wiki/Anti-submarine_warfare "Anti-submarine warfare"), hunting for [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") [submarines](/wiki/Soviet_Navy#Submarines "Soviet Navy") and mostly active in the [GIUK gap](/wiki/GIUK_gap "GIUK gap"). Following the [collapse of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union"), its focus returned to [expeditionary](/wiki/Expeditionary_warfare "Expeditionary warfare") operations.[[6]](#cite_note-10)[[7]](#cite_note-11)[[8]](#cite_note-12) Following a projected £2 billion overspend acknowledged by the MOD in 2025,[[9]](#cite_note-telegraph-20251023-13) the Royal Navy announced it will reduce overseas training in the Indo-Pacific and the Middle East.[[10]](#cite_note-telegraph-20251209-14)

The Royal Navy maintains a fleet of technologically sophisticated ships, submarines, and aircraft, including two [*Queen Elizabeth*-class](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth-class_aircraft_carrier "Queen Elizabeth-class aircraft carrier") [aircraft carriers](/wiki/Aircraft_carriers "Aircraft carriers"), four [*Vanguard*-class](/wiki/Vanguard-class_submarine "Vanguard-class submarine") [ballistic missile submarines](/wiki/Vanguard-class_submarine "Vanguard-class submarine") (which carry the [Trident](/wiki/Trident_(UK_nuclear_programme) "Trident (UK nuclear programme)") [strategic nuclear weapons](/wiki/Strategic_nuclear_weapon "Strategic nuclear weapon")), six [*Astute*-class](/wiki/Astute-class_submarine "Astute-class submarine") nuclear-powered [attack submarines](/wiki/Attack_submarine "Attack submarine"), six [Type 45 guided missile destroyers](/wiki/Type_45_destroyer "Type 45 destroyer"), seven [Type 23 frigates](/wiki/Type_23_frigate "Type 23 frigate"), eight [mine-countermeasure vessels](/wiki/Mine-countermeasure_vessel "Mine-countermeasure vessel") and twenty-six [patrol vessels](/wiki/Patrol_boat "Patrol boat"). As of December 2025, there are [63 active and commissioned ships](/wiki/List_of_active_Royal_Navy_ships "List of active Royal Navy ships") (including submarines as well as one historic ship, [HMS *Victory*](/wiki/HMS_Victory "HMS Victory")) in the Royal Navy, plus 9 ships of the [Royal Fleet Auxiliary](/wiki/Royal_Fleet_Auxiliary "Royal Fleet Auxiliary") (RFA). There are also four [Point-class sealift ships](/wiki/Point-class_sealift_ship "Point-class sealift ship") from the [Merchant Navy](/wiki/Merchant_Navy_(United_Kingdom) "Merchant Navy (United Kingdom)") available to the RFA under a [private finance initiative](/wiki/Private_finance_initiative "Private finance initiative"), while the civilian [Marine Services](/wiki/Serco_Marine_Services "Serco Marine Services") operate auxiliary vessels which further support the Royal Navy in various capacities. The RFA replenishes Royal Navy warships at sea and, since 2024–25, provides the lead elements of the Royal Navy's amphibious warfare capabilities through its three [Bay-class](/wiki/Bay-class_landing_ship "Bay-class landing ship") landing ship vessels. It also works as a force multiplier for the Royal Navy, often doing patrols that frigates used to do.

The Royal Navy is part of His Majesty's Naval Service, which also includes the [Royal Marines](/wiki/Royal_Marines "Royal Marines") and the Royal Fleet Auxiliary. The professional head of the Naval Service is the [First Sea Lord](/wiki/First_Sea_Lord "First Sea Lord") who is a general and member of the [Defence Council of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Defence_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Defence Council of the United Kingdom"). The Defence Council delegates management of the Naval Service to the [Admiralty Board](/wiki/Admiralty_Board_(United_Kingdom) "Admiralty Board (United Kingdom)"), chaired by the [secretary of state for defence](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Defence "Secretary of State for Defence"). The Royal Navy operates from three bases in Britain where commissioned ships and submarines are based: [Portsmouth](/wiki/HMNB_Portsmouth "HMNB Portsmouth"), [Clyde](/wiki/HMNB_Clyde "HMNB Clyde") and [Devonport](/wiki/HMNB_Devonport "HMNB Devonport"), the last being the largest operational naval base in Western Europe, as well as two naval air stations, [RNAS Yeovilton](/wiki/RNAS_Yeovilton_(HMS_Heron) "RNAS Yeovilton (HMS Heron)") and [RNAS Culdrose](/wiki/RNAS_Culdrose_(HMS_Seahawk) "RNAS Culdrose (HMS Seahawk)") where maritime aircraft are based.

## Role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Role")]

The Royal Navy stated its six major roles in umbrella terms in 2017 as:[[11]](#cite_note-15)

* Preventing Conflict – On a global and regional level
* Providing Security At Sea – To ensure the stability of [international trade](/wiki/International_trade "International trade") at sea
* International Partnerships – To help cement the relationship with the United Kingdom's allies (such as [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"))
* Maintaining a Readiness To Fight – To protect the United Kingdom's interests across the globe
* Protecting the Economy – To safeguard vital [trade routes](/wiki/Trade_route "Trade route") to guarantee the United Kingdom's and its allies' economic prosperity at sea
* Providing Humanitarian Aid – To deliver a fast and effective response to global catastrophes

The Royal Navy protects British interests at home and abroad, executing the foreign and defence policies of His Majesty's Government through the exercise of military effect, diplomatic activities and other activities in support of these objectives. It is also a key element of the British contribution to NATO, with a number of ships or aircraft allocated to NATO tasks at any time.[[12]](#cite_note-16) In 2007 core capabilities were described as:[[13]](#cite_note-17)

* Maintenance of the UK Nuclear Deterrent through a policy of [*Continuous at Sea Deterrence*](/wiki/Vanguard_class_submarine "Vanguard class submarine")
* Provision of two medium-scale [maritime task groups](/wiki/Carrier_battle_group "Carrier battle group") with the [Fleet Air Arm](/wiki/Fleet_Air_Arm "Fleet Air Arm")
* Delivery of the UK [Commando](/wiki/British_Commandos "British Commandos") force
* Contribution of assets to the [Joint Aviation Command](/wiki/Joint_Aviation_Command "Joint Aviation Command")
* Maintenance of [standing patrol commitments](/wiki/Standing_Royal_Navy_deployments "Standing Royal Navy deployments")
* Provision of [mine counter measures](/wiki/Minehunter "Minehunter") capability to United Kingdom and allied commitments
* Provision of [hydrographic](/wiki/Hydrography "Hydrography") and [meteorological](/wiki/Meteorological "Meteorological") services deployable worldwide
* Protection of Britain's [Exclusive Economic Zone](/wiki/Exclusive_Economic_Zone "Exclusive Economic Zone")

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main articles: [History of the Royal Navy (before 1707)](/wiki/History_of_the_Royal_Navy_(before_1707) "History of the Royal Navy (before 1707)"), [History of the Royal Navy (after 1707)](/wiki/History_of_the_Royal_Navy_(after_1707) "History of the Royal Navy (after 1707)"), and [Royal Scots Navy](/wiki/Royal_Scots_Navy "Royal Scots Navy")

The English Royal Navy was formally founded in 1546 by [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII"),[[14]](#cite_note-auto-18) though the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") had possessed less-organised naval forces for centuries prior to this.[[15]](#cite_note-19)

The Royal Scots Navy (or Old Scots Navy) had its origins in the Middle Ages until its merger with the English Royal Navy per the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707").[[16]](#cite_note-20)

### Earlier fleets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Earlier fleets")]

Further information: [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") and [First Barons' War](/wiki/First_Barons%27_War "First Barons' War")

During much of the medieval period, fleets or "king's ships" were often established or gathered for specific campaigns or actions, and these would disperse afterwards. These were generally merchant ships enlisted into service. Unlike some European states, England did not maintain a small permanent core of warships in peacetime. England's naval organisation was haphazard and the mobilisation of fleets when war broke out was slow.[[17]](#cite_note-21) Control of the sea only became critical to Anglo-Saxon kings in the 10th century.[[18]](#cite_note-22) In the 11th century, [Æthelred the Unready](/wiki/%C3%86thelred_the_Unready "Æthelred the Unready") had a large fleet built by a national levy.[[19]](#cite_note-23) During the period of [Danish rule](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw") in the 11th century, authorities maintained a standing fleet by taxation, and this continued for a time under [Edward the Confessor](/wiki/Edward_the_Confessor "Edward the Confessor"), who frequently commanded fleets in person.[[20]](#cite_note-24) After the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") in 1066, English naval power waned and England suffered large naval raids from the Vikings.[[21]](#cite_note-25) In 1069, this allowed for the invasion and ravaging of England by Jarl Osborn, brother of [King Svein Estridsson](/wiki/Sweyn_II_of_Denmark "Sweyn II of Denmark"), and his sons.[[22]](#cite_note-26)

The lack of an organised navy came to a head during the [First Barons' War](/wiki/First_Barons%27_War "First Barons' War"), in which [Prince Louis of France](/wiki/Louis_VIII_of_France "Louis VIII of France") invaded England in support of northern barons. With [King John](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England") unable to organise a navy, this meant the French landed at [Sandwich](/wiki/Sandwich,_Kent "Sandwich, Kent") unopposed in April 1216. John's flight to [Winchester](/wiki/Winchester "Winchester") and his death later that year left [the Earl of Pembroke](/wiki/William_Marshal,_1st_Earl_of_Pembroke "William Marshal, 1st Earl of Pembroke") as regent, and he was able to marshal ships to fight the French in the [Battle of Sandwich](/wiki/Battle_of_Sandwich_(1217) "Battle of Sandwich (1217)") in 1217 – one of the first major English battles at sea.[[23]](#cite_note-27) The outbreak of the [Hundred Years War](/wiki/Hundred_Years_War "Hundred Years War") emphasised the need for an English fleet. French plans for an invasion of England failed when [Edward III of England](/wiki/Edward_III_of_England "Edward III of England") destroyed the French fleet in the [Battle of Sluys](/wiki/Battle_of_Sluys "Battle of Sluys") in 1340.[[24]](#cite_note-28) England's naval forces could not prevent frequent raids on the south-coast ports by the French and their allies. Such raids halted only with the occupation of northern France by [Henry V](/wiki/Henry_V_of_England "Henry V of England").[[25]](#cite_note-29)

A [Scottish fleet](/wiki/Royal_Scots_Navy "Royal Scots Navy") existed by the reign of [William the Lion](/wiki/William_the_Lion "William the Lion") in the late 12th century.[[26]](#cite_note-Tytler1829pp309-10-30) In the early 13th century there was a resurgence of Viking naval power in the region. The Vikings clashed with Scotland over control of the isles[[27]](#cite_note-31) though [Alexander III](/wiki/Alexander_III_of_Scotland "Alexander III of Scotland") was ultimately successful in asserting Scottish control.[[28]](#cite_note-Macquarrie2004p153-32) The Scottish fleet was of particular import in repulsing English forces in the early 14th century.[[29]](#cite_note-Rodger1997pp74-90-33)

### Age of Sail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Age of Sail")]

See also: [Age of Sail](/wiki/Age_of_Sail "Age of Sail"), [History of the Royal Navy (before 1707)](/wiki/History_of_the_Royal_Navy_(before_1707) "History of the Royal Navy (before 1707)"), and [Tudor navy](/wiki/Tudor_navy "Tudor navy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/English_Ships_and_the_Spanish_Armada%2C_August_1588_RMG_BHC0262.jpg/250px-English_Ships_and_the_Spanish_Armada%2C_August_1588_RMG_BHC0262.jpg)](/wiki/File:English_Ships_and_the_Spanish_Armada,_August_1588_RMG_BHC0262.jpg)

A late 16th-century portrait of the [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada") battling Royal Navy warships

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/HMS_Victory_-_bow.jpg/250px-HMS_Victory_-_bow.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Victory_-_bow.jpg)

[HMS *Victory*](/wiki/HMS_Victory "HMS Victory"), [Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson,_1st_Viscount_Nelson "Horatio Nelson, 1st Viscount Nelson")'s [flagship](/wiki/Flagship "Flagship") at [Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar"), is still a [commissioned](/wiki/Ship_commissioning "Ship commissioning") Royal Navy ship, although she is now permanently kept in [dry-dock](/wiki/Dry-dock "Dry-dock").

A standing "Navy Royal",[[14]](#cite_note-auto-18) with its own secretariat, [dockyards](/wiki/Royal_Naval_Dockyards "Royal Naval Dockyards") and a permanent core of purpose-built warships, emerged during the reign of Henry VIII.[[30]](#cite_note-34) Under [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England"), England became involved in a [war with Spain](/wiki/Anglo-Spanish_War_(1585%E2%80%931604) "Anglo-Spanish War (1585–1604)"), which saw privately owned vessels combining with the Queen's ships in highly profitable raids against [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") commerce and colonies.[[31]](#cite_note-35) The Royal Navy was then used in 1588 to repulse the [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada"), but the [English Armada](/wiki/English_Armada "English Armada") was lost the next year. In 1603, the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns") created a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") between England and Scotland. While the two remained distinct sovereign states for a further century, the two navies increasingly fought as a single force. During the early 17th century, England's relative naval power deteriorated until [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") undertook a major programme of shipbuilding. His [methods of financing](/wiki/Ship_money "Ship money") the fleet contributed to the outbreak of the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), and the [abolition of the monarchy](/wiki/Interregnum "Interregnum").[[32]](#cite_note-36)

The [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England") replaced many names and symbols in the new Commonwealth Navy, associated with royalty and the [high church](/wiki/High_church "High church"), and expanded it to become the most powerful in the world.[[33]](#cite_note-37)[[4]](#cite_note-rodgercommand-8) The fleet was quickly tested in the [First Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Dutch_War "First Anglo-Dutch War") (1652–1654) and the [Anglo-Spanish War (1654–1660)](/wiki/Anglo-Spanish_War_(1654%E2%80%931660) "Anglo-Spanish War (1654–1660)"), which saw the British [conquest of Jamaica](/wiki/Invasion_of_Jamaica "Invasion of Jamaica") and successful [attacks on Spanish treasure fleets](/wiki/Battle_of_C%C3%A1diz_(1656) "Battle of Cádiz (1656)"). The 1660 [Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration") saw [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") rename the Royal Navy again, and started use of the prefix [HMS](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Ship "His Majesty's Ship"). The Navy remained a national institution and not a possession of [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") as it had been before.[[34]](#cite_note-38) Following the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") of 1688, England joined the [War of the Grand Alliance](/wiki/War_of_the_Grand_Alliance "War of the Grand Alliance") which marked the end of [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France")'s brief pre-eminence at sea and the beginning of an enduring British supremacy which would help with the creation of the British Empire.[[35]](#cite_note-39)

In 1707, the [Scottish navy](/wiki/Scottish_navy "Scottish navy") was united with the English Royal Navy. On Scottish men-of-war, the cross of St Andrew was replaced with the Union Jack. On English ships, the red, white, or blue ensigns had the St George's Cross of England removed from the canton, and the combined crosses of the Union flag put in its place.[[36]](#cite_note-40) Throughout the 18th and 19th centuries, the Royal Navy was the largest maritime force in the world,[[37]](#cite_note-41) maintaining superiority in financing, tactics, training, organisation, social cohesion, hygiene, logistical support and warship design.[[38]](#cite_note-42) The peace settlement following the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession") (1702–1714) granted Britain [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") and [Menorca](/wiki/Menorca "Menorca"), providing the Navy with [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") bases. The expansion of the Royal Navy would encourage the [British colonisation of the Americas](/wiki/British_colonization_of_the_Americas "British colonization of the Americas"), with [British (North) America](/wiki/British_America "British America") becoming a vital source of [timber](/wiki/Lumber "Lumber") for the Royal Navy.[[39]](#cite_note-43) There was a defeat during the frustrated [siege of Cartagena de Indias](/wiki/Battle_of_Cartagena_de_Indias "Battle of Cartagena de Indias") in 1741. A new French [attempt to invade Britain](/wiki/Planned_French_invasion_of_Britain_(1759) "Planned French invasion of Britain (1759)") was thwarted by the defeat of their escort fleet in the extraordinary [Battle of Quiberon Bay](/wiki/Battle_of_Quiberon_Bay "Battle of Quiberon Bay") in 1759, fought in dangerous conditions.[[40]](#cite_note-44) In 1762, the resumption of hostilities with [Spain](/wiki/History_of_Spain_(1700%E2%80%931810) "History of Spain (1700–1810)") led to the [British capture of Manila](/wiki/British_occupation_of_Manila "British occupation of Manila") and of [Havana](/wiki/Siege_of_Havana "Siege of Havana"), along with a Spanish fleet sheltering there.[[41]](#cite_note-45) British naval supremacy could however be challenged still in this period by coalitions of other nations, as seen in the [American War of Independence](/wiki/American_War_of_Independence "American War of Independence"). The [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") was [allied to France](/wiki/Franco-American_alliance "Franco-American alliance"), and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and Spain were also at war with Britain. In the [Battle of the Chesapeake](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Chesapeake "Battle of the Chesapeake") in 1781, the British fleet failed to lift the French blockade, resulting in the surrender of an entire British army at [Yorktown](/wiki/Siege_of_Yorktown_(1781) "Siege of Yorktown (1781)").[[42]](#cite_note-46)

The [French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_and_Napoleonic_Wars "French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars") (1793–1801, 1803–1814 & 1815) saw the Royal Navy reach a peak of efficiency, dominating the navies of all Britain's adversaries, which spent most of the war blockaded in port. Although the largest and potentially most dangerous mutinies in the history of the Royal Navy took place at [Spithead and the Nore](/wiki/Spithead_and_Nore_mutinies "Spithead and Nore mutinies") in spring 1797, British domination at sea was not threatened.[[43]](#cite_note-47) Under [Lord Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson,_1st_Viscount_Nelson "Horatio Nelson, 1st Viscount Nelson"), the navy defeated the combined Franco-Spanish fleet at [Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar") (1805).[[44]](#cite_note-48) Ships of the line and even frigates, as well as manpower, were prioritised for the naval war in Europe, however, leaving only smaller vessels on the [North America Station](/wiki/North_America_Station "North America Station") and other less active stations, and a heavy reliance upon impressed labour. This would result in problems countering large, well-armed United States Navy frigates which outgunned Royal Naval vessels in single-opponent actions, as well as United States privateers, when the [American War of 1812](/wiki/American_War_of_1812 "American War of 1812") broke out concurrent with the war against Napoleonic France and its allies. The Royal Navy still enjoyed a numerical advantage over the former colonists on the Atlantic, and from its base in Bermuda it blockaded the [Atlantic seaboard](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") of the United States throughout the war and carried out (with Royal Marines, [Colonial Marines](/wiki/Corps_of_Colonial_Marines "Corps of Colonial Marines"), [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"), and [Board of Ordnance](/wiki/Board_of_Ordnance "Board of Ordnance") military corps units) various amphibious operations, most notably the [Chesapeake campaign](/wiki/Chesapeake_campaign "Chesapeake campaign"). On the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes"), however, the United States Navy established an advantage.[[45]](#cite_note-49)

### Splendid isolation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Splendid isolation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/De_Engels-Nederlandse_vloot_in_de_Baai_van_Algiers_ter_ondersteuning_van_het_ultimatum_tot_vrijlating_van_blanke_slaven%2C_26_augustus_1816._Rijksmuseum_SK-A-1377.jpeg/500px-thumbnail.jpeg)](/wiki/File:De_Engels-Nederlandse_vloot_in_de_Baai_van_Algiers_ter_ondersteuning_van_het_ultimatum_tot_vrijlating_van_blanke_slaven,_26_augustus_1816._Rijksmuseum_SK-A-1377.jpeg)

Bombardment of [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers "Algiers") in [north Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") by the Anglo-Dutch fleet in an attempt to stop the [Barbary slave trade](/wiki/Barbary_slave_trade "Barbary slave trade"), 27 August 1816

In 1860, [Albert, Prince Consort](/wiki/Prince_Albert_of_Saxe-Coburg_and_Gotha "Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha"), wrote to the foreign secretary [John Russell, 1st Earl Russell](/wiki/John_Russell,_1st_Earl_Russell "John Russell, 1st Earl Russell") with his concern about "a perfect disgrace to our country, and particularly to the [Admiralty](/wiki/Admiralty_(United_Kingdom) "Admiralty (United Kingdom)")". The stated shipbuilding policy of the British monarchy was to take advantage of technological change and so be able to deploy a new [weapons system](/wiki/Weapons_system "Weapons system") that could defend British interests before other national and imperial resources are reasonably mobilized. Nevertheless, British taxpayers scrutinized progress in modernizing the Royal Navy so as to ensure, that taxpayers' money is not wasted.[[46]](#cite_note-50)

Between 1815 and 1914, the Royal Navy saw little serious action, owing to the [absence of any opponent strong enough](/wiki/Pax_Britannica "Pax Britannica") to challenge its dominance. It did not suffer the drastic cutbacks the various military forces underwent in the period of economic austerity that followed the end of the Napoleonic Wars and the American War of 1812 (when the British Army and the Board of Ordnance military corps were cut back, weakening garrisons around the Empire, the Militia became a paper tiger, and the Volunteer Force and Fencible units disbanded, though the Yeomanry was maintained as a back-up to the police). Britain relied, throughout the 19th century and the first half of the 20th century, on imperial fortress colonies (originally [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda"), Gibraltar, [Halifax (Nova Scotia)](/wiki/Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax, Nova Scotia"), and [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta")). These areas permitted Britain to control the Atlantic Ocean and Mediterranean Sea. Control of military forces in Nova Scotia passed to the new Government of Canada after the 1867 [Confederation of Canada](/wiki/Confederation_of_Canada "Confederation of Canada") and control of the naval dockyard in Halifax, Nova Scotia was transferred to the Government of Canada in 1905, five years prior to the establishment of the [Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Navy "Royal Canadian Navy"). Prior to the 1920s, it was presumed that the only navies that could challenge the Royal Navy belonged to nations on the Atlantic Ocean or its connected seas, despite the growth of the Imperial Russian and United States Pacific fleets during the latter half of the 19th Century.[[47]](#cite_note-51)[[48]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Second_taking_of_Chusan.jpg/250px-Second_taking_of_Chusan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Second_taking_of_Chusan.jpg)

The [capture of Chusan](/wiki/Capture_of_Chusan_(1841) "Capture of Chusan (1841)") during the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") on 1 October 1841

Britain relied on Malta, in the Mediterranean Sea, to project power to the Indian Ocean and western Pacific Ocean via the Suez Canal after its completion in 1869. It relied on friendship and common interests between Britain and the United States (which controlled transit through the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal"), completed in 1914) during and after the First World War, and on Bermuda, to project power the length of the western Atlantic, including the Caribbean Sea and Gulf of Mexico. The area controlled from Bermuda (and Halifax until 1905) had been part of the North America Station, until the 1820s, which then absorbed the Jamaica Station to become the [North America and West Indies Station](/wiki/North_America_and_West_Indies_Station "North America and West Indies Station"). After the First World War, this formation assumed responsibility for the eastern Pacific Ocean and the western South Atlantic and was known as the *America and West Indies Station* until 1956.[[49]](#cite_note-53)[[50]](#cite_note-54) In 1921, due to the ambitions of Imperial Japan and the threat of the [Imperial Japanese Navy](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Navy "Imperial Japanese Navy"), it was decided to construct the [Singapore Naval Base](/wiki/Singapore_Naval_Base "Singapore Naval Base").[[51]](#cite_note-55)

During this period, naval warfare underwent a comprehensive transformation, brought about by [steam propulsion](/wiki/Steam_engine "Steam engine"), metal ship construction, and explosive munitions. Despite having to completely replace its war fleet, the Navy managed to maintain its overwhelming advantage over all potential rivals. Owing to British leadership in the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), the country enjoyed unparalleled shipbuilding capacity and financial resources, which ensured that no rival could take advantage of these revolutionary changes to negate the British advantage in ship numbers.[[52]](#cite_note-56)

In 1889, Parliament passed the [Naval Defence Act](/wiki/Naval_Defence_Act_1889 "Naval Defence Act 1889"), which formally adopted the 'two-power standard', which stipulated that the Royal Navy should maintain a number of battleships at least equal to the combined strength of the next two largest navies.[[53]](#cite_note-57) The end of the 19th century saw structural changes and older vessels were scrapped or placed into reserve, making funds and manpower available for newer ships. The launch of [HMS *Dreadnought*](/wiki/HMS_Dreadnought_(1906) "HMS Dreadnought (1906)") in 1906 rendered all existing battleships obsolete.[[54]](#cite_note-58) The transition at this time from coal to fuel-oil for boiler firing would encourage Britain to expand their foothold in former [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") territories in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), especially [Iraq](/wiki/Mandatory_Iraq "Mandatory Iraq").[[55]](#cite_note-59)

### Exploration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Exploration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Cook_Three_Voyages_59.png/500px-Cook_Three_Voyages_59.png)](/wiki/File:Cook_Three_Voyages_59.png)

The routes of Royal Navy Captain [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook")'s three voyages

Further information: [Challenger expedition](/wiki/Challenger_expedition "Challenger expedition"), [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook"), [North-West Passage](/wiki/North-West_Passage "North-West Passage"), [Second voyage of HMS Beagle](/wiki/Second_voyage_of_HMS_Beagle "Second voyage of HMS Beagle"), and [Vancouver Expedition](/wiki/Vancouver_Expedition "Vancouver Expedition")

The Royal Navy played an historic role in several great global explorations of science and discovery.[[56]](#cite_note-60) Beginning in the 18th century many great voyages were commissioned often in co-operation with the [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society"), such as the [Northwest Passage expedition of 1741](/wiki/Northwest_Passage_expedition_of_1741 "Northwest Passage expedition of 1741"). [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") led three great voyages, with goals such as discovering [Terra Australis](/wiki/Terra_Australis "Terra Australis"), observing the [Transit of Venus](/wiki/Transit_of_Venus "Transit of Venus") and searching for the elusive [North-West Passage](/wiki/North-West_Passage "North-West Passage"), these voyages are considered to have contributed to world knowledge and science.[[57]](#cite_note-Franklin1837-61) In the late 18th century, during a [four year voyage](/wiki/Vancouver_Expedition "Vancouver Expedition") Captain [George Vancouver](/wiki/George_Vancouver "George Vancouver") made detailed maps of the western coastline of [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America").[[58]](#cite_note-62)

In the 19th century, [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin") made further contributions to science during the [second voyage of HMS Beagle](/wiki/Second_voyage_of_HMS_Beagle "Second voyage of HMS Beagle").[[59]](#cite_note-RMG-63) The [Ross expedition](/wiki/Ross_expedition "Ross expedition") to the Antarctic made several important discoveries in [biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology") and [zoology](/wiki/Zoology "Zoology").[[60]](#cite_note-64) Several of the Royal Navy's voyages ended in disaster such as those of [Franklin](/wiki/Franklin%27s_lost_expedition "Franklin's lost expedition") and [Scott](/wiki/Terra_Nova_Expedition "Terra Nova Expedition").[[61]](#cite_note-65) Between 1872 and 1876 [HMS *Challenger*](/wiki/HMS_Challenger_(1858) "HMS Challenger (1858)") undertook the first global marine research expedition, the [*Challenger* expedition](/wiki/Challenger_expedition "Challenger expedition").[[62]](#cite_note-66)

### World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: World War I")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/HMS_Warspite_and_HMS_Malaya_during_the_battle_of_Jutland.jpg/500px-HMS_Warspite_and_HMS_Malaya_during_the_battle_of_Jutland.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Warspite_and_HMS_Malaya_during_the_battle_of_Jutland.jpg)

[HMS *Warspite*](/wiki/HMS_Warspite_(03) "HMS Warspite (03)") and [*Malaya*](/wiki/HMS_Malaya "HMS Malaya"), seen from [*Valiant*](/wiki/HMS_Valiant_(1914) "HMS Valiant (1914)") at the [Battle of Jutland](/wiki/Battle_of_Jutland "Battle of Jutland") in 1916

Further information: [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I")

Main article: [Royal Navy during World War I](/wiki/Royal_Navy_during_World_War_I "Royal Navy during World War I")

During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the Royal Navy's strength was mostly deployed at home in the [Grand Fleet](/wiki/Grand_Fleet "Grand Fleet"), confronting the German [High Seas Fleet](/wiki/High_Seas_Fleet "High Seas Fleet") across the North Sea. Several inconclusive clashes took place between them, chiefly the [Battle of Jutland](/wiki/Battle_of_Jutland "Battle of Jutland") in 1916.[[63]](#cite_note-67) The British fighting advantage proved insurmountable, leading the High Seas Fleet to abandon any attempt to challenge British dominance.[[64]](#cite_note-68) The Royal Navy under [John Jellicoe](/wiki/John_Jellicoe,_1st_Earl_Jellicoe "John Jellicoe, 1st Earl Jellicoe") also tried to avoid combat and remained in port at [Scapa Flow](/wiki/Scapa_Flow "Scapa Flow") for much of the war.[[65]](#cite_note-69) This was contrary to widespread prewar expectations that in the event of a Continental conflict Britain would primarily provide naval support to the [Entente Powers](/wiki/Triple_Entente "Triple Entente") while sending at most only a small ground army. Nevertheless, the Royal Navy played an important role in securing the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") and the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"), notably ferrying the entire [British Expeditionary Force](/wiki/British_Expeditionary_Force_(World_War_I) "British Expeditionary Force (World War I)") to the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)") at the beginning of the war without the loss of a single life.[[66]](#cite_note-70)

The Royal Navy nevertheless remained active in other theatres, most notably in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"), where they waged the [Dardanelles](/wiki/Dardanelles_campaign "Dardanelles campaign") and [Gallipoli campaigns](/wiki/Gallipoli_campaign "Gallipoli campaign") in 1914 and 1915. British cruisers hunted down German commerce raiders across the world's oceans in 1914 and 1915, including the battles of [Coronel](/wiki/Battle_of_Coronel "Battle of Coronel"), [Falklands Islands](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Falkland_Islands "Battle of the Falkland Islands"), [Cocos](/wiki/Battle_of_Cocos "Battle of Cocos"), and [Rufiji Delta](/wiki/Battle_of_Rufiji_Delta "Battle of Rufiji Delta"), among others.[[67]](#cite_note-71)

### Interwar period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Interwar period")]

At the end of World War I, the Royal Navy remained by far the world's most powerful navy,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] larger than the [U.S. Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") and [French Navy](/wiki/French_Navy "French Navy") combined, and over twice as large as the [Imperial Japanese Navy](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Navy "Imperial Japanese Navy") and [Royal Italian Navy](/wiki/Regia_Marina "Regia Marina") combined. Its former primary competitor, the Imperial German Navy, was [destroyed at the end of the war](/wiki/Scuttling_of_the_German_fleet_at_Scapa_Flow "Scuttling of the German fleet at Scapa Flow").[[68]](#cite_note-72) In the [inter-war period](/wiki/Interwar_Britain "Interwar Britain"), the Royal Navy was stripped of much of its power. The [Washington](/wiki/Washington_Naval_Treaty "Washington Naval Treaty") and [London Naval Treaties](/wiki/London_Naval_Treaty "London Naval Treaty") imposed the scrapping of some capital ships and limitations on new construction.[[69]](#cite_note-73)

The lack of an imperial fortress in the region of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean"), and the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") was always to be a weakness throughout the 19th century as the former North American colonies that had become the United States of America had multiplied towards the Pacific Coast of North America, and the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") and [Japanese Empire](/wiki/Japanese_Empire "Japanese Empire") both had ports on the Pacific and had begun building large, modern fleets which went to war with each other in 1904. Britain's reliance on Malta, via the Suez Canal, as the nearest Imperial fortress was improved, relying on amity and common interests that developed between Britain and the United States during and after World War I, by the completion of the Panama Canal in 1914, allowing the cruisers based in Bermuda to more easily and rapidly reach the eastern Pacific Ocean (after the war, the Royal Navy's Bermuda-based *North America and West Indies Station* was consequently re-designated the *America and West Indies station*, including a [South American](/wiki/South_America "South America") division. The rising power and increasing belligerence of the [Japanese Empire](/wiki/Japanese_empire "Japanese empire") after World War I, however, resulted in the construction of the [Singapore Naval Base](/wiki/Singapore_Naval_Base "Singapore Naval Base"), which was completed in 1938, less than four years before hostilities with [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") did commence during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[70]](#cite_note-74)

In 1931, the [Invergordon Mutiny](/wiki/Invergordon_Mutiny "Invergordon Mutiny") took place in the [Atlantic Fleet](/wiki/Atlantic_Fleet_(United_Kingdom) "Atlantic Fleet (United Kingdom)") over the [National Government](/wiki/National_Government_(1931%E2%80%931935) "National Government (1931–1935)")'s proposed 25% pay cut, which was eventually reduced to 10%.[[71]](#cite_note-75) [International tensions](/wiki/International_relations_(1919%E2%80%931939) "International relations (1919–1939)") increased in the mid-1930s and the [re-armament of the Royal Navy](/wiki/British_re-armament "British re-armament") was well under way by 1938. In addition to new construction, several existing old [battleships](/wiki/Battleship "Battleship"), [battlecruisers](/wiki/Battlecruiser "Battlecruiser") and [heavy cruisers](/wiki/Heavy_cruiser "Heavy cruiser") were reconstructed, and [anti-aircraft weaponry](/wiki/Anti-aircraft_warfare "Anti-aircraft warfare") reinforced, while new technologies, such as [ASDIC](/wiki/ASDIC "ASDIC"), [Huff-Duff](/wiki/Huff-Duff "Huff-Duff") and [hydrophones](/wiki/Hydrophones "Hydrophones"), were developed.[[72]](#cite_note-76)

### World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: World War II")]

Further information: [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")

Main article: [Royal Navy during the Second World War](/wiki/Royal_Navy_during_the_Second_World_War "Royal Navy during the Second World War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/The_King_Pays_4-day_Visit_To_the_Home_Fleet._18_To_21_March_1943%2C_at_Scapa_Flow%2C_the_King%2C_Wearing_the_Uniform_of_An_Admiral_of_the_Fleet%2C_Paid_a_4-day_Visit_To_the_Home_Fleet._A15117.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_King_Pays_4-day_Visit_To_the_Home_Fleet._18_To_21_March_1943,_at_Scapa_Flow,_the_King,_Wearing_the_Uniform_of_An_Admiral_of_the_Fleet,_Paid_a_4-day_Visit_To_the_Home_Fleet._A15117.jpg)

King [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") visiting the [Home Fleet](/wiki/Home_Fleet "Home Fleet") based at [Scapa Flow](/wiki/Scapa_Flow "Scapa Flow"), March 1943

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/British_navy_frogman.jpg/250px-British_navy_frogman.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_navy_frogman.jpg)

Royal Navy commando in 1945

At the start of World War II in 1939, the Royal Navy was still the largest in the world, with over 1,400 vessels.[[73]](#cite_note-77)[[74]](#cite_note-78) The Royal Navy provided critical cover during [Operation Dynamo](/wiki/Operation_Dynamo "Operation Dynamo"), the British evacuations from [Dunkirk](/wiki/Battle_of_Dunkirk "Battle of Dunkirk"), and as the ultimate deterrent to a [German invasion of Britain](/wiki/Operation_Sea_Lion "Operation Sea Lion") during the following four months. The [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe") under [Hermann Göring](/wiki/Hermann_G%C3%B6ring "Hermann Göring") attempted to gain [air supremacy](/wiki/Air_supremacy "Air supremacy") over [southern England](/wiki/Southern_England "Southern England") in the [Battle of Britain](/wiki/Battle_of_Britain "Battle of Britain") in order to neutralise the [Home Fleet](/wiki/Home_Fleet "Home Fleet"), but faced stiff resistance from the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force").[[75]](#cite_note-79) The Royal Navy's [Fleet Air Arm](/wiki/Fleet_Air_Arm "Fleet Air Arm") assisted the Royal Air Force, which was suffering from personnel shortages, during the battle.[[76]](#cite_note-80) The Luftwaffe bombing offensive during the [Kanalkampf](/wiki/Kanalkampf "Kanalkampf") phase of the battle targeted naval convoys and bases in order to lure large concentrations of RAF fighters into [attrition warfare](/wiki/Attrition_warfare "Attrition warfare").[[77]](#cite_note-81) At [Taranto](/wiki/Battle_of_Taranto "Battle of Taranto"), [Admiral Cunningham](/wiki/Andrew_Cunningham,_1st_Viscount_Cunningham_of_Hyndhope "Andrew Cunningham, 1st Viscount Cunningham of Hyndhope") commanded a fleet that launched the first all-aircraft naval attack in history. The Royal Navy suffered heavy [losses](/wiki/List_of_Royal_Navy_losses_in_World_War_II "List of Royal Navy losses in World War II") in the first two years of the war. Over 3,000 people were lost when the converted [troopship](/wiki/Troopship "Troopship") *[Lancastria](/wiki/RMS_Lancastria "RMS Lancastria")* was sunk in June 1940, the greatest maritime disaster in Britain's history.[[78]](#cite_note-82) The Navy's most critical struggle was the [Battle of the Atlantic](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Atlantic "Battle of the Atlantic") defending Britain's vital North American commercial supply lines against [U-boat](/wiki/U-boat "U-boat") attack. A traditional [convoy system](/wiki/Convoy "Convoy") was instituted from the start of the war, but German submarine tactics, based on group attacks by "[wolf-packs](/wiki/Wolfpack_(naval_tactic) "Wolfpack (naval tactic)")", were much more effective than in the previous war, and the threat remained serious for well over three years.[[79]](#cite_note-83)

### Cold War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Cold War")]

Further information: [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Polaris_missile_launch_from_HMS_Revenge_%28S27%29_1983.JPEG/250px-Polaris_missile_launch_from_HMS_Revenge_%28S27%29_1983.JPEG)](/wiki/File:Polaris_missile_launch_from_HMS_Revenge_(S27)_1983.JPEG)

A [Polaris missile](/wiki/UGM-27_Polaris "UGM-27 Polaris") is fired from the submerged British [ballistic missile submarine](/wiki/Ballistic_missile_submarine "Ballistic missile submarine") [HMS *Revenge*](/wiki/HMS_Revenge_(S27) "HMS Revenge (S27)") on 9 June 1983

After World War II, the decline of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") and the [economic hardships in Britain](/wiki/Economic_history_of_the_United_Kingdom#1945–2001 "Economic history of the United Kingdom") forced the reduction in the size and capability of the Royal Navy. The United States Navy instead took on the role of global naval power. Governments since have faced increasing budgetary pressures, partly due to the [increasing cost of weapons systems](/wiki/Upward_Spiral "Upward Spiral").[[80]](#cite_note-84)

In 1981, Defence Secretary [John Nott](/wiki/John_Nott "John Nott") had advocated and initiated a [series of cutbacks to the Navy](/wiki/1981_Defence_White_Paper "1981 Defence White Paper").[[81]](#cite_note-85) The [Falklands War](/wiki/Falklands_War "Falklands War") however proved a need for the Royal Navy to regain an expeditionary and [littoral](/wiki/Littoral "Littoral") capability which, with its resources and structure at the time, would prove difficult. At the beginning of the 1980s, the Royal Navy was a force focused on [blue-water](/wiki/Blue-water_navy "Blue-water navy") [anti-submarine warfare](/wiki/Anti-submarine_warfare "Anti-submarine warfare"). Its purpose was to search for and destroy [Soviet submarines](/wiki/Soviet_Navy#Submarines "Soviet Navy") in the North Atlantic, and to operate the nuclear deterrent submarine force. The navy received its first nuclear weapons with the introduction of the first of the [*Resolution*-class submarines](/wiki/Resolution-class_submarine "Resolution-class submarine") armed with the [Polaris missile](/wiki/Polaris_missile "Polaris missile").[[82]](#cite_note-86)

### Post-Cold War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Post-Cold War")]

Following the conclusion of the collapse of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and the end of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") in 1991, the Royal Navy began to experience a gradual decline in its fleet size in accordance with the changed strategic environment it operated in. While new and more capable ships are continually brought into service, such as the [*Queen Elizabeth*-class aircraft carriers](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth-class_aircraft_carrier "Queen Elizabeth-class aircraft carrier"), [*Astute*-class submarines](/wiki/Astute-class_submarine "Astute-class submarine"), and [Type 45](/wiki/Type_45_destroyer "Type 45 destroyer") [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyer "Destroyer"), the total number of ships and submarines operated has continued to steadily reduce. This has caused considerable debate about the size of the Royal Navy. A 2013 report found that the Royal Navy was already too small, and that Britain would have to depend on her allies if her territories were attacked.[[83]](#cite_note-87)

The Royal Navy was responsible for training the fledgling [Iraqi Navy](/wiki/Iraqi_Navy "Iraqi Navy") and securing Iraq's oil terminals following the cessation of hostilities in the country. The Iraqi Training and Advisory Mission (Navy) ([Umm Qasr](/wiki/Umm_Qasr "Umm Qasr")), headed by a Royal Navy captain, has been responsible for the former duty whilst Commander Task Force Iraqi Maritime, a Royal Navy commodore, was responsible for the latter.[[84]](#cite_note-88)[[85]](#cite_note-89) The mission ended in May 2011.[[86]](#cite_note-90)

The financial costs attached to nuclear deterrence, including [Trident missile](/wiki/Trident_(missile) "Trident (missile)") upgrades and replacements, have become an increasingly significant issue for the navy.[[87]](#cite_note-91)

## Assets and resources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Assets and resources")]

See also: [Future of the Royal Navy](/wiki/Future_of_the_Royal_Navy "Future of the Royal Navy")

### Personnel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Personnel")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/BRNC-Dartmouth.jpg/250px-BRNC-Dartmouth.jpg)](/wiki/File:BRNC-Dartmouth.jpg)

[Britannia Royal Naval College](/wiki/Britannia_Royal_Naval_College "Britannia Royal Naval College") in [Dartmouth, Devon](/wiki/Dartmouth,_Devon "Dartmouth, Devon")

[HMS *Raleigh*](/wiki/HMS_Raleigh_(shore_establishment) "HMS Raleigh (shore establishment)") at [Torpoint, Cornwall](/wiki/Torpoint,_Cornwall "Torpoint, Cornwall"), is the basic training facility for newly enlisted ratings. [Britannia Royal Naval College](/wiki/Britannia_Royal_Naval_College "Britannia Royal Naval College") in [Dartmouth, Devon](/wiki/Dartmouth,_Devon "Dartmouth, Devon") is the initial officer training establishment for the Royal Navy. Personnel are divided into a [warfare branch](/wiki/Officer_Corps_of_the_Royal_Navy "Officer Corps of the Royal Navy"), which includes Warfare Officers (previously named seamen officers) and [Naval Aviators](/wiki/Fleet_Air_Arm "Fleet Air Arm"),[[88]](#cite_note-92) as well other branches including the [Royal Naval Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Naval_Engineers "Royal Naval Engineers"), [Royal Navy Medical Branch](/wiki/Royal_Navy_Medical_Branch "Royal Navy Medical Branch"), and [Logistics Officers](/wiki/Logistics_Officer "Logistics Officer") (previously named [Supply Officers](/wiki/Supply_Officer "Supply Officer")). Present-day officers and ratings have several different [uniforms](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_Royal_Navy "Uniforms of the Royal Navy"); some are designed to be worn aboard ship, others ashore or in ceremonial duties. Women began to join the Royal Navy in 1917 with the formation of the [Women's Royal Naval Service](/wiki/Women%27s_Royal_Naval_Service "Women's Royal Naval Service") (WRNS), which was disbanded after the end of the First World War in 1919. It was revived in 1939, and the WRNS continued until disbandment in 1993, as a result of the decision to fully integrate women into the structures of the Royal Navy. Women now serve in all sections of the Royal Navy including the [Royal Marines](/wiki/Royal_Marines "Royal Marines").[[89]](#cite_note-93)

In August 2019, the [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)") published figures showing that the Royal Navy and Royal Marines had 29,090 full-time trained personnel compared with a target of 30,600.[[90]](#cite_note-94) In 2023, it was reported that the Royal Navy was experiencing significant recruiting challenges with a net drop of some 1,600 personnel (4 percent of the force) from mid-2022 to mid-2023. This was posing a significant problem in the ability of the navy to meet its commitments.[[91]](#cite_note-95)

In December 2019 the [First Sea Lord](/wiki/First_Sea_Lord "First Sea Lord"), [Admiral](/wiki/Admiral_(Royal_Navy) "Admiral (Royal Navy)") [Tony Radakin](/wiki/Tony_Radakin "Tony Radakin"), outlined a proposal to reduce the number of Rear-Admirals at [Navy Command](/wiki/Navy_Command_(Ministry_of_Defence) "Navy Command (Ministry of Defence)") by five.[[92]](#cite_note-96) The fighting arms (excluding [Commandant General Royal Marines](/wiki/Commandant_General_Royal_Marines "Commandant General Royal Marines")) would be reduced to [commodore](/wiki/Commodore_(Royal_Navy) "Commodore (Royal Navy)") (1-star) rank and the surface flotillas would be combined. Training would be concentrated under the [Fleet Commander](/wiki/Fleet_Commander "Fleet Commander").[[93]](#cite_note-97)

In 1952, Royal Navy ratings belonged to one of eleven branches: Seaman (may specialise as Boom Defence Rating; Gunnery Rating; Physical and Recreational Training Instructor; Quartermaster; Radar Plotter; Sailmaker; Surveying Recorder; Torpedo and Submarine Detection Rating); Naval Aviation (Aircraft Artificer; Aircraft Handler; Aircraft Mechanic (Airframes); Aircraft Mechanic (Engines); Aircraft Mechanic (Ordnance); Meteorological Observer; Photographer; Safety Equipment Rating); Communications (Signalman; Telegraphist); Shipwright (Shipwright Artificer); Electrical (Electrician; Electrician (Air); Electrical Artificer; Electrical Artificer (Air); Radio Electrical Artificer; Radio Electrical Artificer (Air); Radio Electrician; Radio Electrician (Air)); Engineering (Engine Room Artificer; Stoker Mechanic); Ordnance (Ordnance Artificer); Regulating (Master-at-Arms; Patrol Rating); Supply and Secretarial (Cook; Steward; Stores Rating; Writer); Medical (Laboratory Assistant; Operating Room Assistant; Physiotherapist (Masseur); Radiographer; Sanitary Inspector; Sick Berth Attendant); and Dental (Dental Surgery Attendant).[[94]](#cite_note-Chaffe-98)

### Surface fleet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Surface fleet")]

See also: [List of active Royal Navy ships](/wiki/List_of_active_Royal_Navy_ships "List of active Royal Navy ships")

Main article: [Royal Navy Surface Fleet](/wiki/Royal_Navy_Surface_Fleet "Royal Navy Surface Fleet")

#### Aircraft carriers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Aircraft carriers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/HMS_Queen_Elizabeth_%28R08%29_underway_during_trials_with_HMS_Sutherland_%28F81%29_and_HMS_Iron_Duke_%28F234%29_on_28_June_2017_%2845162784%29.jpg/250px-HMS_Queen_Elizabeth_%28R08%29_underway_during_trials_with_HMS_Sutherland_%28F81%29_and_HMS_Iron_Duke_%28F234%29_on_28_June_2017_%2845162784%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Queen_Elizabeth_(R08)_underway_during_trials_with_HMS_Sutherland_(F81)_and_HMS_Iron_Duke_(F234)_on_28_June_2017_(45162784).jpg)

[HMS *Queen Elizabeth*](/wiki/HMS_Queen_Elizabeth_(R08) "HMS Queen Elizabeth (R08)"), a [*Queen Elizabeth*-class](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth-class_aircraft_carrier "Queen Elizabeth-class aircraft carrier") aircraft carrier, on sea trials in June 2017

The Royal Navy has two *Queen Elizabeth*-class aircraft carriers. Each carrier cost £3.2 billion and has an empty load displacement of 65,000 tonnes (64,000 long tons; 72,000 short tons),[[95]](#cite_note-99) rising up to an estimated 80,600 tonnes (79,300 long tons; 88,800 short tons) full load displacement.[[96]](#cite_note-100) Both are intended to operate the [short take-off and landing](/wiki/V/STOL "V/STOL") (STOVL) variant of the [F-35 Lightning II](/wiki/F-35_Lightning_II "F-35 Lightning II"). The first, [HMS *Queen Elizabeth*](/wiki/HMS_Queen_Elizabeth_(R08) "HMS Queen Elizabeth (R08)"), commenced flight trials in 2018. *Queen Elizabeth* began sea trials in June 2017, was commissioned later that year, and entered service in 2020,[[97]](#cite_note-101) while the second, [HMS *Prince of Wales*](/wiki/HMS_Prince_of_Wales_(R09) "HMS Prince of Wales (R09)"), began sea trials on 22 September 2019, was commissioned in December 2019 and was declared operational as of October 2021.[[98]](#cite_note-102)[[99]](#cite_note-103)[[100]](#cite_note-facts-104)[[101]](#cite_note-105)[[102]](#cite_note-106) The aircraft carriers form a central part of the [UK Carrier Strike Group](/wiki/UK_Carrier_Strike_Group "UK Carrier Strike Group") alongside escorts and support ships.[[103]](#cite_note-assemble-107)

#### Amphibious warfare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Amphibious warfare")]

Until 2024/25, the Royal Navy's amphibious capability consisted of two [landing platform docks](/wiki/Landing_platform_dock "Landing platform dock") ([HMS *Albion*](/wiki/HMS_Albion_(L14) "HMS Albion (L14)") and [HMS *Bulwark*](/wiki/HMS_Bulwark_(L15) "HMS Bulwark (L15)")). While their primary role was to conduct amphibious warfare, they were also deployed for [humanitarian aid](/wiki/Humanitarian_aid "Humanitarian aid") missions.[[104]](#cite_note-human-108) Both vessels were in reserve as of 2024[[105]](#cite_note-109) and in November 2024, the newly elected Labour government indicated that they would in fact be retired from service completely by March 2025.[[106]](#cite_note-110) While second-line amphibious capabilities remained within the Royal Fleet Auxiliary, the future of the navy's amphibious capability was now in question.[[107]](#cite_note-auto2-111)

#### Clearance diving

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Clearance diving")]

The Royal Navy [clearance diving](/wiki/Clearance_divers "Clearance divers") unit, the Fleet Diving Squadron, was reorganised and renamed the Diving and Threat Exploitation Group in 2022. The group consists of five squadrons: Alpha, Bravo, Charlie, Delta, and Echo.[[108]](#cite_note-112)[[109]](#cite_note-113) The Royal Navy has a separate unit with divers the [special forces](/wiki/Special_forces "Special forces") unit the [Special Boat Service](/wiki/Special_Boat_Service "Special Boat Service").[[110]](#cite_note-ArmyMuseum-114)

#### Escort fleet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Escort fleet")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Duncan_%287899777334%29.jpg/250px-Duncan_%287899777334%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Duncan_(7899777334).jpg)

[HMS *Duncan*](/wiki/HMS_Duncan_(D37) "HMS Duncan (D37)"), the [Type 45](/wiki/Type_45_destroyer "Type 45 destroyer") [guided missile destroyer](/wiki/Guided_missile_destroyer "Guided missile destroyer")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Type_23_frigate_HMS_KENT_at_Sea%2C_south_of_the_Isle_of_Wight_MOD_45158148.jpg/250px-Type_23_frigate_HMS_KENT_at_Sea%2C_south_of_the_Isle_of_Wight_MOD_45158148.jpg)](/wiki/File:Type_23_frigate_HMS_KENT_at_Sea,_south_of_the_Isle_of_Wight_MOD_45158148.jpg)

[HMS *Kent*](/wiki/HMS_Kent_(F78) "HMS Kent (F78)"), the [Type 23 frigate](/wiki/Type_23_frigate "Type 23 frigate") designed for anti-submarine warfare

The escort fleet comprises [guided missile destroyers](/wiki/Guided_missile_destroyer "Guided missile destroyer") and [frigates](/wiki/Frigates "Frigates") and is the traditional workhorse of the Navy.[[111]](#cite_note-MODRNinfo-115) As of December 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit) there are six Type 45 destroyers and seven [Type 23 frigates](/wiki/Type_23_frigate "Type 23 frigate") in commission. Among their primary roles is to provide escort for the larger capital ships—protecting them from air, surface and subsurface threats. Other duties include undertaking the Royal Navy's [standing deployments](/wiki/Standing_Royal_Navy_deployments "Standing Royal Navy deployments") across the globe, which often consists of: counter-narcotics, anti-piracy missions and providing humanitarian aid.[[104]](#cite_note-human-108)

The Type 45 is primarily designed for anti-aircraft and anti-missile warfare and the Royal Navy describe the destroyer's mission as "to shield the Fleet from air attack".[[112]](#cite_note-116) They are equipped with the [PAAMS](/wiki/PAAMS "PAAMS") (also known as Sea Viper) integrated anti-aircraft warfare system which incorporates the sophisticated [SAMPSON](/wiki/SAMPSON "SAMPSON") and [S1850M](/wiki/S1850M "S1850M") long range radars and the [Aster 15 and 30](/wiki/Aster_(missile_family) "Aster (missile family)") missiles.[[113]](#cite_note-BAEType45-117)

Sixteen Type 23 frigates were delivered to the Royal Navy, with the final vessel, [HMS *St Albans*](/wiki/HMS_St_Albans_(F83) "HMS St Albans (F83)"), commissioned in June 2002. However, the 2004 [Delivering Security in a Changing World](/wiki/Delivering_Security_in_a_Changing_World "Delivering Security in a Changing World") review announced that three frigates would be [paid off](/wiki/Paid_off "Paid off") as part of a cost-cutting exercise, and these were subsequently sold to the [Chilean Navy](/wiki/Chilean_Navy "Chilean Navy").[[114]](#cite_note-SellType23-118) The 2010 [Strategic Defence and Security Review](/wiki/Strategic_Defence_and_Security_Review_2010 "Strategic Defence and Security Review 2010") announced that the remaining 13 Type 23 frigates would eventually be replaced by the [Type 26 Frigate](/wiki/Type_26_Frigate "Type 26 Frigate"),[[115]](#cite_note-119) with the incremental retirement of the remaining Type 23s commencing in 2021. The [Strategic Defence and Security Review 2015](/wiki/Strategic_Defence_and_Security_Review_2015 "Strategic Defence and Security Review 2015") reduced the procurement of Type 26 to eight with five [Type 31e frigates](/wiki/Type_31_frigate "Type 31 frigate") also to be procured.[[116]](#cite_note-120)

As part of the Maritime Electronic Warfare System Integrated Capability (MEWSIC) programme, the [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)") awarded a £100 million contract in 2022 to a consortium led by [Babcock International](/wiki/Babcock_International "Babcock International"), with [QinetiQ](/wiki/Qinetiq "Qinetiq") and [Elbit Systems UK](/wiki/Elbit_Systems_UK "Elbit Systems UK"), a subsidiary of the Israel-based defence technology company [Elbit Systems](/wiki/Elbit_Systems "Elbit Systems").[[117]](#cite_note-121)[[118]](#cite_note-122) The 13-year programme is intended to upgrade the Royal Navy's electronic warfare capabilities, particularly against threats such as [anti-ship missiles](/wiki/Anti-ship_missile "Anti-ship missile"). By 2025, testing of the new systems was underway, with full integration planned for platforms including the Type 45, [Type 26](/wiki/Type_26_frigate "Type 26 frigate"), and [Type 31](/wiki/Type_31_frigate "Type 31 frigate") frigates.[[119]](#cite_note-123)

#### Mine countermeasure vessels (MCMV)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Mine countermeasure vessels (MCMV)")]

There are two classes of traditional [MCMVs](/wiki/Mine_countermeasure_vessel "Mine countermeasure vessel") in the Royal Navy: one [*Sandown*-class](/wiki/Sandown-class_minehunter "Sandown-class minehunter") [minehunter](/wiki/Minehunter "Minehunter") and six [Hunt-class](/wiki/Hunt-class_mine_countermeasures_vessel "Hunt-class mine countermeasures vessel") [mine countermeasures vessels](/wiki/Mine_countermeasures_vessel "Mine countermeasures vessel"). Crewed mine countermeasures vessels are in the process of being incrementally replaced by autonomous systems, such as the [Arcims-class](/wiki/Sea-class_workboat#Autonomous_minehunter "Sea-class workboat") and vessels being procured from [Thales defence systems](/wiki/Thales_Group "Thales Group"). It is planned that these systems may operate from a range of vessels, including so-called "motherships". The first of these 'motherships', [HMS Stirling Castle](/wiki/RFA_Stirling_Castle "RFA Stirling Castle"), was transferred to the Royal Navy from the Royal Fleet Auxiliary in 2025. The Hunt-class vessels combine the separate roles of the traditional [minesweeper](/wiki/Minesweeper "Minesweeper") and the active minehunter in one hull. If required, crewed MCMVs can also take on the role of offshore patrol vessels.[[120]](#cite_note-124)

#### Offshore patrol vessels (OPV)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Offshore patrol vessels (OPV)")]

A fleet of eight [River-class offshore patrol vessels](/wiki/River-class_offshore_patrol_vessel "River-class offshore patrol vessel") are in service with the Royal Navy. The three Batch 1 ships of the class serve in U.K. waters in a sovereignty and fisheries protection role while the five Batch 2 ships are forward-deployed on a long-term basis to Gibraltar, the Caribbean, the [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands") and the [Indo-Pacific region](/wiki/Indo-Pacific "Indo-Pacific").[[121]](#cite_note-125) The vessel MV *Grampian Frontier* is leased from Scottish-based company North Star Shipping for patrol duties around the [British Indian Ocean Territory](/wiki/British_Indian_Ocean_Territory "British Indian Ocean Territory"). However, she is not in commission with the Royal Navy.[[122]](#cite_note-sites.google.com-126)

In December 2019, the modified Batch 1 River-class vessel, [HMS *Clyde*](/wiki/HMS_Clyde_(P257) "HMS Clyde (P257)"), was decommissioned, with the Batch 2 [HMS *Forth*](/wiki/HMS_Forth_(P222) "HMS Forth (P222)") taking over duties as the Falkland Islands patrol ship.[[123]](#cite_note-127)[[124]](#cite_note-128)

#### Survey ships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Survey ships")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/HMS_Protector_Assisting_the_Antarctic_Community._MOD_45156397.jpg/330px-HMS_Protector_Assisting_the_Antarctic_Community._MOD_45156397.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Protector_Assisting_the_Antarctic_Community._MOD_45156397.jpg)

[HMS *Protector*](/wiki/HMS_Protector_(A173) "HMS Protector (A173)"), a Royal Navy [Antarctic](/wiki/British_Antarctica "British Antarctica") [patrol ship](/wiki/Patrol_ship "Patrol ship")

[HMS *Protector*](/wiki/HMS_Protector_(A173) "HMS Protector (A173)") is a dedicated [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica") patrol ship that fulfils the nation's mandate to provide support to the [British Antarctic Survey](/wiki/British_Antarctic_Survey "British Antarctic Survey") (BAS).[[125]](#cite_note-129) [HMS *Scott*](/wiki/HMS_Scott_(H131) "HMS Scott (H131)") is an ocean survey vessel and at 13,500 tonnes is one of the largest ships in the Navy. As of 2018, the newly commissioned [HMS *Magpie*](/wiki/HMS_Magpie_(H130) "HMS Magpie (H130)") also undertakes survey duties at sea.[[126]](#cite_note-130) The Royal Fleet Auxiliary plans to introduce two new [Multi-Role Ocean Surveillance Ships](/wiki/Multi-Role_Ocean_Surveillance_Ship "Multi-Role Ocean Surveillance Ship"), in part to protect [undersea cables](/wiki/Submarine_cable "Submarine cable") and [gas pipelines](/wiki/Pipeline "Pipeline") and partly to compensate for the withdrawal of all ocean-going survey vessels from Royal Navy service.[[127]](#cite_note-MROSS-131) The first of these vessels, [RFA *Proteus*](/wiki/RFA_Proteus "RFA Proteus"), entered service in October 2023.[[128]](#cite_note-132)

#### Royal Fleet Auxiliary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Royal Fleet Auxiliary")]

Main article: [Royal Fleet Auxiliary](/wiki/Royal_Fleet_Auxiliary "Royal Fleet Auxiliary")

The Royal Fleet Auxiliary (RFA) provides support to the Royal Navy at sea in several capacities. For fleet replenishment, it deploys one [Fleet Solid Support Ship](/wiki/Fleet_Solid_Support_Ship "Fleet Solid Support Ship") (in reserve as of late 2024) and four [Tide-class](/wiki/Tide-class_tanker "Tide-class tanker") [fleet tankers](/wiki/Fleet_tanker "Fleet tanker").[[129]](#cite_note-133)

Three [amphibious transport docks](/wiki/Amphibious_transport_dock "Amphibious transport dock") are also incorporated within its fleet. These are known as the [Bay-class](/wiki/Bay-class_landing_ship "Bay-class landing ship") landing ships, of which four were introduced in 2006–2007, but one was sold to the [Royal Australian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Navy "Royal Australian Navy") in 2011.[[130]](#cite_note-134) In November 2006, the then First Sea Lord Admiral Sir [Jonathon Band](/wiki/Jonathon_Band "Jonathon Band") described the Royal Fleet Auxiliary vessels as "a major uplift in the Royal Navy's war fighting capability".[[131]](#cite_note-135)

In February 2023, a commercial vessel was also acquired to act as a [Multi-Role Ocean Surveillance Ship](/wiki/Multi-Role_Ocean_Surveillance_Ship "Multi-Role Ocean Surveillance Ship") (MROSS) for the protection of critical seabed infrastructure and other tasks. She entered service as [RFA *Proteus*](/wiki/RFA_Proteus "RFA Proteus").[[132]](#cite_note-136) An additional vessel, [RFA *Stirling Castle*](/wiki/HMS_Stirling_Castle_(M01) "HMS Stirling Castle (M01)"), was acquired in 2023 to act as a [mothership](/wiki/Mothership "Mothership") for autonomous minehunting systems.[[133]](#cite_note-137) This ship was transferred from the Royal Fleet Auxiliary to the Royal Navy in 2025.[[134]](#cite_note-138)

#### Other ships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Other ships")]

The Royal Navy also includes a number of smaller non-commissioned assets such as the [Sea-class workboats](/wiki/Sea-class_workboat "Sea-class workboat"). The Fleet Experimentation Squadron, within the Disruptive Capabilities and Technologies Office, operates a number of vessels and systems to trial high technology military capabilities. These include vessels such as XV *Excalibur*, an Extra Large Uncrewed Submarine christened in 2025.[[135]](#cite_note-139)[[136]](#cite_note-140)[[137]](#cite_note-141) On 29 July 2022, the Royal Navy christened a new experimental ship, [XV *Patrick Blackett*](/wiki/XV_Patrick_Blackett "XV Patrick Blackett"), which it aims to use as a testbed for autonomous systems. Whilst the ship flies the [Blue Ensign](/wiki/Blue_Ensign "Blue Ensign"), it is crewed by Royal Navy personnel and will participate in Royal Navy and NATO exercises.[[138]](#cite_note-142)[[139]](#cite_note-TD1-143)

### Submarine Service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Submarine Service")]

Main article: [Royal Navy Submarine Service](/wiki/Royal_Navy_Submarine_Service "Royal Navy Submarine Service")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/HMS_Astute_Arrives_at_Faslane_for_the_First_Time_MOD_45150806.jpg/250px-HMS_Astute_Arrives_at_Faslane_for_the_First_Time_MOD_45150806.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Astute_Arrives_at_Faslane_for_the_First_Time_MOD_45150806.jpg)

[HMS *Astute*](/wiki/HMS_Astute_(S119) "HMS Astute (S119)"), the first [*Astute*-class](/wiki/Astute-class_submarine "Astute-class submarine") [nuclear submarine](/wiki/Nuclear_submarine "Nuclear submarine")

The Submarine Service is the [submarine](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine") based element of the Royal Navy. It is sometimes referred to as the "*Silent Service*",[[140]](#cite_note-144) as the submarines are generally required to operate undetected. Founded in 1901, the service made history in 1982 when, during the Falklands War, [HMS *Conqueror*](/wiki/HMS_Conqueror_(S48) "HMS Conqueror (S48)") became the first nuclear-powered submarine to sink a surface ship, [ARA *General Belgrano*](/wiki/ARA_General_Belgrano "ARA General Belgrano"). Today, all of the Royal Navy's submarines are [nuclear-powered](/wiki/Nuclear_marine_propulsion "Nuclear marine propulsion").[[141]](#cite_note-145)

#### Ballistic missile submarines (SSBN)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Ballistic missile submarines (SSBN)")]

The Royal Navy operates four [*Vanguard*-class](/wiki/Vanguard-class_submarine "Vanguard-class submarine") [ballistic missile submarines](/wiki/Ballistic_missile_submarine "Ballistic missile submarine") displacing nearly 16,000 tonnes and equipped with Trident II missiles (armed with [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_and_the_United_Kingdom "Nuclear weapons and the United Kingdom")) and heavyweight [Spearfish torpedoes](/wiki/Spearfish_torpedo "Spearfish torpedo"), to carry out Operation Relentless, the United Kingdom's Continuous At Sea Deterrent (CASD). The UK government has committed to replace these submarines with four new [*Dreadnought*-class submarines](/wiki/Dreadnought-class_submarine "Dreadnought-class submarine"), which will enter service in the "early 2030s" to maintain this capability.[[142]](#cite_note-theyworkforyou1-146)[[143]](#cite_note-147)

#### Fleet submarines (SSN)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Fleet submarines (SSN)")]

As of September 2025, six [fleet submarines](/wiki/Fleet_submarine "Fleet submarine") of the [*Astute-*class](/wiki/Astute-class_submarine "Astute-class submarine") are in commission with the previous [Trafalgar class submarines](/wiki/Trafalgar_class_submarine "Trafalgar class submarine") having been withdrawn from service.[[144]](#cite_note-auto1-148) Another *Astute-*class fleet submarine is scheduled to enter service by the late-2020s.[[145]](#cite_note-Thomas-149)[[146]](#cite_note-direct.gov.uk-150)

The *Astute-*class, at 7,400 tonnes,[[147]](#cite_note-151) carry both [Tomahawk](/wiki/Tomahawk_(missile_family) "Tomahawk (missile family)") land-attack missiles and [Spearfish](/wiki/Spearfish_torpedo "Spearfish torpedo") torpedoes. In 2025, [HMS *Agamemnon*](/wiki/HMS_Agamemnon_(S124) "HMS Agamemnon (S124)") was the most recent *Astute*-class boat to be commissioned,[[144]](#cite_note-auto1-148) though it will be at least another 12 to 18 months before she is fully operational.[[145]](#cite_note-Thomas-149)[[148]](#cite_note-152)[[149]](#cite_note-commissioning-153)

### Fleet Air Arm

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Fleet Air Arm")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/UK_F-35B_Lightning_II_MOD_45157752.jpg/250px-UK_F-35B_Lightning_II_MOD_45157752.jpg)](/wiki/File:UK_F-35B_Lightning_II_MOD_45157752.jpg)

[F-35B](/wiki/F-35_Lightning_II "F-35 Lightning II") aircraft are operated from the [*Queen Elizabeth*-class aircraft carriers](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth-class_aircraft_carrier "Queen Elizabeth-class aircraft carrier")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Waddington_Westland_Merlin_HM2_ZH850_80_060713.jpg/250px-Waddington_Westland_Merlin_HM2_ZH850_80_060713.jpg)](/wiki/File:Waddington_Westland_Merlin_HM2_ZH850_80_060713.jpg)

[AW-101 Merlin](/wiki/AgustaWestland_AW101 "AgustaWestland AW101") HM2 anti-submarine warfare helicopter

Main article: [Fleet Air Arm](/wiki/Fleet_Air_Arm "Fleet Air Arm")

The Fleet Air Arm (FAA) is recognised as one of the five fighting arms of the Royal Navy and oversees the [operations of naval aircraft](/wiki/Naval_aviation "Naval aviation").[[150]](#cite_note-154)

Established officially within the Royal Navy in 1939,[[151]](#cite_note-155) the Fleet Air Arm has been instrumental in naval combat, offering aerial support for fleet missions, performing [reconnaissance tasks](/wiki/Aerial_reconnaissance "Aerial reconnaissance"), and executing offensive operations against both maritime and land-based objectives.[[152]](#cite_note-156)

The contemporary Fleet Air Arm functions as an essential element of the Royal Navy’s combat capabilities. Its main responsibilities encompass:

* Carrier Strike Operations: Operating the fixed-wing [F-35B Lightning II](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin_F-35_Lightning_II "Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II"), the FAA equips the Royal Navy with a long-range strike capability. These aircraft are launched from the [*Queen Elizabeth*-class](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth-class_aircraft_carrier "Queen Elizabeth-class aircraft carrier") [aircraft carriers](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier").[[153]](#cite_note-157)
* Maritime Surveillance and Reconnaissance: [AW-101 Merlin](/wiki/AgustaWestland_AW101 "AgustaWestland AW101") HM2 and [AW-159 Wildcat](/wiki/AgustaWestland_AW159 "AgustaWestland AW159") HMA2 helicopters are utilised for [anti-submarine warfare](/wiki/Anti-submarine_warfare "Anti-submarine warfare") (ASW), [anti-surface warfare](/wiki/Anti-surface_warfare "Anti-surface warfare") (ASuW), and intelligence-gathering missions.[[154]](#cite_note-158)[[155]](#cite_note-159)
* Search and Rescue (SAR) and Humanitarian Missions: The FAA has a longstanding tradition of engaging in [Search and Rescue](/wiki/Search_and_Rescue "Search and Rescue") operations and disaster relief efforts, frequently in partnership with international allies.[[156]](#cite_note-160)[[157]](#cite_note-161)
* Littoral Strike Support: The FAA plays a vital role in [amphibious warfare](/wiki/Amphibious_warfare "Amphibious warfare"), delivering essential air support for Royal Marines and other ground forces. The FAA operates the AW-101 Merlin HC4 and AW-159 Wildcat AH1 (in support of [UK Commando Force](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Commando_Force "United Kingdom Commando Force")) as the [Commando Helicopter Force](/wiki/Commando_Helicopter_Force "Commando Helicopter Force").[[158]](#cite_note-162)[[159]](#cite_note-163)

Pilots designated for rotary wing service train under [No. 1 Flying Training School](/wiki/No._1_Flying_Training_School_RAF "No. 1 Flying Training School RAF") (1 FTS)[[160]](#cite_note-164) at RAF Shawbury.[[161]](#cite_note-165)

### Royal Marines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Royal Marines")]

Main article: [Royal Marines](/wiki/Royal_Marines "Royal Marines")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Royal_Marines_in_Sangin_MOD_45151554.jpg/250px-Royal_Marines_in_Sangin_MOD_45151554.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Marines_in_Sangin_MOD_45151554.jpg)

[Royal Marines](/wiki/Royal_Marines "Royal Marines") in [Sangin](/wiki/Sangin "Sangin") in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") in 2010

The Royal Marines are an amphibious, specialised [light infantry](/wiki/Light_infantry "Light infantry") force of [commandos](/wiki/Commandos "Commandos"), capable of deploying at short notice in support of [His Majesty's Government](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Government "His Majesty's Government")'s military and diplomatic objectives overseas.[[162]](#cite_note-166) The Royal Marines are organised into a highly mobile light infantry brigade ([UK Commando Force](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Commando_Force "United Kingdom Commando Force")) and 7 commando units[[163]](#cite_note-167) including 47 Commando (Raiding Group) Royal Marines, [43 Commando Fleet Protection Group Royal Marines](/wiki/43_Commando_Fleet_Protection_Group_Royal_Marines "43 Commando Fleet Protection Group Royal Marines") and a company strength commitment to the [Special Forces Support Group](/wiki/Special_Forces_Support_Group "Special Forces Support Group"). The Corps operates in all environments and climates, though particular expertise and training is spent on [amphibious warfare](/wiki/Amphibious_warfare "Amphibious warfare"), [Arctic warfare](/wiki/Arctic_warfare "Arctic warfare"), [mountain warfare](/wiki/Mountain_warfare "Mountain warfare"), [expeditionary warfare](/wiki/Expeditionary_warfare "Expeditionary warfare") and commitment to the UK's [Rapid Reaction Force](/wiki/Rapid_Reaction_Force "Rapid Reaction Force"). The Royal Marines are also the primary source of personnel for the Royal Navy's special forces unit the Special Boat Service (SBS).[[164]](#cite_note-168)[[110]](#cite_note-ArmyMuseum-114)

The Corps operates its own [fleet of landing and other craft](/wiki/List_of_active_Royal_Marines_military_watercraft "List of active Royal Marines military watercraft"), and also incorporates the [Royal Marines Band Service](/wiki/Royal_Marines_Band_Service "Royal Marines Band Service"), the musical wing of the Royal Navy.[[165]](#cite_note-169)

The Royal Marines have seen constant action since they were formed, often fighting beside the British Army; including in the Seven Years' War, the Napoleonic Wars, the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War"), World War I and World War II. Most has been offshore away from the United Kingdom.
