In recent times, the Corps has been deployed in the [Falklands War](/wiki/Falklands_War "Falklands War"), the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War"), the [Bosnian War](/wiki/Bosnian_War "Bosnian War"), the [Kosovo War](/wiki/Kosovo_War "Kosovo War"), the [Sierra Leone Civil War](/wiki/Sierra_Leone_Civil_War "Sierra Leone Civil War"), the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War") and the [War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)"). The Royal Marines have international ties with allied marine forces, particularly the [United States Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") and the [Netherlands Marine Corps](/wiki/Netherlands_Marine_Corps "Netherlands Marine Corps")/Korps Mariniers.[[166]](#cite_note-mod.uk-170)[[167]](#cite_note-royalnavy.mod.uk-171)

## Command, control and organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Command, control and organisation")]

The [Sovereign](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom") is the [Commander-in-chief of the British Armed Forces](/wiki/Commander-in-chief_of_the_British_Armed_Forces "Commander-in-chief of the British Armed Forces").[[168]](#cite_note-172) The titular head of the Royal Navy is the [Lord High Admiral of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Lord_High_Admiral_of_the_United_Kingdom "Lord High Admiral of the United Kingdom"), a position which was held by [Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh](/wiki/Prince_Philip,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh") from 2011 until his death in 2021 and which remains in the reigning monarch's gift.[[169]](#cite_note-GHR-173)[[170]](#cite_note-BBC2022-174) The position had been held by Queen Elizabeth II from 1964 to 2011.[[171]](#cite_note-175) The [prime minister of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime minister of the United Kingdom") is *de facto* commander-in-chief of the armed forces, with the [Secretary of State for Defence](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Defence "Secretary of State for Defence") the minister permanently responsible. The professional head of the Naval Service is the [First Sea Lord and Chief of Naval Staff](/wiki/First_Sea_Lord_and_Chief_of_Naval_Staff "First Sea Lord and Chief of Naval Staff") (1SL/CNS), a member of the [Defence Council of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Defence_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Defence Council of the United Kingdom"). The Defence Council delegates management of the Naval Service to the [Admiralty Board](/wiki/Admiralty_Board_(United_Kingdom) "Admiralty Board (United Kingdom)"), chaired by the secretary of state for defence, which directs the [Navy Board](/wiki/Navy_Board "Navy Board"), a sub-committee of the Admiralty Board comprising only naval officers and Ministry of Defence (MOD) civil servants. These are all based in the [Ministry of Defence Main Building](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_Main_Building_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Defence Main Building (United Kingdom)") in London, where the First Sea Lord & CNS is supported by the Naval Staff.[[172]](#cite_note-176)

### Organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Organisation")]

The Fleet Commander has responsibility for the provision of ships, submarines and aircraft ready for any operations that the Government requires. Fleet Commander exercises his authority through the [Navy Command Headquarters](/wiki/Navy_Command_Headquarters "Navy Command Headquarters"), based at [HMS *Excellent*](/wiki/HMS_Excellent_(shore_establishment) "HMS Excellent (shore establishment)") in Portsmouth. Day-to-day operational command of ships, aircraft and Royal Marines is split: large deployed operations such as [United Kingdom Carrier Strike Group 21](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Carrier_Strike_Group_21 "United Kingdom Carrier Strike Group 21") (Operation Fortis) often fall under the [Permanent Joint Headquarters](/wiki/Permanent_Joint_Headquarters "Permanent Joint Headquarters") of the United Kingdom's armed forces, at [Northwood](/wiki/Northwood,_London "Northwood, London") in the northwest suburbs of London; while across the same site at the [Northwood Headquarters](/wiki/Northwood_Headquarters "Northwood Headquarters"), [Commander Operations (Royal Navy)](/wiki/Commander_Operations_(Royal_Navy) "Commander Operations (Royal Navy)") supervises individual ships on independent activities and the patrolling [Vanguard-class submarine](/wiki/Vanguard-class_submarine "Vanguard-class submarine"). The UK retains control of the NATO functional [Allied Maritime Command](/wiki/Allied_Maritime_Command "Allied Maritime Command"), also on the same site.[[173]](#cite_note-177)

The Royal Navy was the first of the three armed forces to combine the personnel and training command, under the Principal Personnel Officer, with the last remaining operational command, combining the Headquarters of the Commander-in-Chief, Fleet and Naval Home Command into a single organisation, Fleet Command, in 2005 and becoming Navy Command in 2008. The [Second Sea Lord](/wiki/Second_Sea_Lord "Second Sea Lord") acts as the Principal Personnel Officer.[[174]](#cite_note-178) Previously, Flag Officer Sea Training was part of the list of top senior appointments in Navy Command, however, as part of the Navy Command Transformation Programme, the post has reduced from Rear-Admiral to Commodore, renamed as [Commander Fleet Operational Sea Training](/wiki/Commander_Fleet_Operational_Sea_Training "Commander Fleet Operational Sea Training").[[175]](#cite_note-179)

The Naval Command senior appointments are:[[176]](#cite_note-180)[[177]](#cite_note-181)

The Commandant General Royal Marines was previously a major-general's post and charged with leading amphibious warfare operations. Since [Lieutenant General](/wiki/Lieutenant_General "Lieutenant General") [Robert Magowan](/wiki/Robert_Magowan "Robert Magowan") was appointed for the second time the post is an additional responsibility for a senior Royal Marine holding other duties. The current CG RM is General [Sir Gwyn Jenkins](/wiki/Gwyn_Jenkins "Gwyn Jenkins"), the [First Sea Lord](/wiki/First_Sea_Lord "First Sea Lord").[[178]](#cite_note-182)

Intelligence support to fleet operations is provided by intelligence sections at the various headquarters and from MOD [Defence Intelligence](/wiki/Defence_Intelligence "Defence Intelligence"), renamed from the Defence Intelligence Staff in early 2010.[[179]](#cite_note-183)

### Current deployments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Current deployments")]

Main article: [Standing Royal Navy deployments](/wiki/Standing_Royal_Navy_deployments "Standing Royal Navy deployments")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/RN_Flotilla_45154692.jpg/250px-RN_Flotilla_45154692.jpg)](/wiki/File:RN_Flotilla_45154692.jpg)

The Royal Navy's presence in the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf") typically includes a Type 45 destroyer and a squadron of minehunters supported by an RFA [Bay-class](/wiki/Bay-class_landing_ship "Bay-class landing ship") mothership.

The Royal Navy is currently deployed in different areas of the world, including some standing Royal Navy deployments. These include several home tasks as well as overseas deployments. The Navy is deployed in the Mediterranean as part of standing NATO deployments including mine countermeasures and NATO Maritime Group 2. In both the North and South Atlantic, RN vessels are patrolling. There is always a *River*-class patrol vessel deployed in the South Atlantic.[[180]](#cite_note-184)

The Royal Navy operates a [Response Force Task Group](/wiki/Response_Force_Task_Group "Response Force Task Group") (a product of the 2010 Strategic Defence and Security Review), which is poised to respond globally to short-notice tasking across a range of defence activities, such as non-combatant evacuation operations, disaster relief, humanitarian aid or amphibious operations. In 2011, the first deployment of the task group occurred under the name 'COUGAR 11' which saw them transit through the Mediterranean where they took part in multinational amphibious exercises before moving further east through the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal") for further exercises in the Indian Ocean.[[181]](#cite_note-185)[[182]](#cite_note-186)

In the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf"), and until 2025/26, the RN has sustained commitments in support of both national and coalition efforts to stabilise the region. [Operation Kipion](/wiki/Operation_Kipion "Operation Kipion") had been the navy's primary activity in the Gulf region. The Royal Navy has also contributed to the US-led [Combined Maritime Forces](/wiki/Combined_Maritime_Forces "Combined Maritime Forces") in the Gulf in partnership with the United States.[[183]](#cite_note-187) The UK Maritime Component Commander, overseer of all of His Majesty's warships in the Persian Gulf and surrounding waters, is also deputy commander of the Combined Maritime Forces.[[184]](#cite_note-188) However, as of 2025/26, these forces were withdrawn and it was unclear how or whether they would be replaced.[[185]](#cite_note-189)[[186]](#cite_note-190)

The Royal Navy contributes to standing NATO formations and maintains forces as part of the [NATO Response Force](/wiki/NATO_Response_Force "NATO Response Force"). The RN also has a long-standing commitment to supporting the [Five Powers Defence Arrangements](/wiki/Five_Powers_Defence_Arrangements "Five Powers Defence Arrangements") countries and occasionally deploys to the Far East as a result.[[187]](#cite_note-191) This deployment typically consists of a frigate and a [survey vessel](/wiki/Echo-class_survey_ship_(2002) "Echo-class survey ship (2002)"), operating separately. [Operation Atalanta](/wiki/Operation_Atalanta "Operation Atalanta"), the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union")'s anti-[piracy](/wiki/Piracy "Piracy") operation in the Indian Ocean, is permanently commanded by a senior Royal Navy or Royal Marines officer at [Northwood Headquarters](/wiki/Northwood_Headquarters "Northwood Headquarters") and the navy contributes ships to the operation.[[188]](#cite_note-192)

From 2015, the Royal Navy also re-formed its UK Carrier Strike Group (UKCSG) after it was disbanded in 2011 due to the retirement of [HMS *Ark Royal*](/wiki/HMS_Ark_Royal_(R07) "HMS Ark Royal (R07)") and Harrier GR9s.[[189]](#cite_note-NN-193)[[190]](#cite_note-SRNA-194) The *Queen Elizabeth*-class aircraft carriers form the central part of this formation, supported by various escorts and support ships, with the aim to facilitate carrier-enabled power projection.[[191]](#cite_note-CEPP-195) The UKCSG first assembled at sea in October 2020 as part of a rehearsal for its first operational deployment in 2021.[[103]](#cite_note-assemble-107)

In 2019, the Royal Navy announced the formation of two [Littoral Response Groups](/wiki/Littoral_Response_Group "Littoral Response Group") as part of a transformation of its amphibious forces. These [forward-based](/wiki/Forward_basing "Forward basing") special operations-capable task groups were to be rapidly-deployable and able to carry out a range of tasks within the littoral, including raids and precision strikes. The first one, based in Europe, became operational in 2021, whilst the second was to be ready for deployment in the Indo-Pacific from 2023. They centred around the two navy amphibious assault ships, amphibious auxiliary ships from the Royal Fleet Auxiliary, elements from the Royal Marines and supporting units.[[192]](#cite_note-LRG1-196) However, in November 2024, with the government's decision to retire the *Albion*-class assault ships along with the subsequent retirement of RFA *Argus*, these plans were no longer viable.[[107]](#cite_note-auto2-111)

### Locations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Locations")]

Main article: [List of Royal Navy shore establishments](/wiki/List_of_Royal_Navy_shore_establishments "List of Royal Navy shore establishments")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/HMNB_Clyde.jpg/250px-HMNB_Clyde.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMNB_Clyde.jpg)

[HMNB Clyde](/wiki/HMNB_Clyde "HMNB Clyde"), [Faslane](/wiki/Faslane "Faslane"), home of the [*Vanguard*-class](/wiki/Vanguard-class_submarine "Vanguard-class submarine") submarines

Historically the Navy had a number of geographical commands, each under a Commander-in-Chief, and often informally referred to as "stations". Over 300 years to 1971 these commands were repeatedly reduced in number, until they were merged into a single entity. The former stations of the Royal Navy included the [East Indies Station](/wiki/East_Indies_Station "East Indies Station") (1744–1831); [East Indies and China Station](/wiki/East_Indies_and_China_Station "East Indies and China Station") (1832–1865); East Indies Station (1865–1913); Egypt and East Indies Station (1913–1918); East Indies Station (1918–1941). Later the Eastern Fleet became the East Indies Fleet. In 1952, after the Second World War ended, the East Indies Fleet became the [Far East Fleet](/wiki/Far_East_Fleet_(United_Kingdom) "Far East Fleet (United Kingdom)").[[193]](#cite_note-197) In 1971 the final merger into a single fleet took place with Far East Fleet merged into the larger single formation under the orders of the [Commander-in-Chief Fleet](/wiki/Commander-in-Chief_Fleet "Commander-in-Chief Fleet") (CINCFLEET).[[194]](#cite_note-198)

The Royal Navy currently operates from three bases in the United Kingdom where commissioned ships are based; [Portsmouth](/wiki/HMNB_Portsmouth "HMNB Portsmouth"), [Clyde](/wiki/HMNB_Clyde "HMNB Clyde") and [Devonport](/wiki/HMNB_Devonport "HMNB Devonport"), [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth "Plymouth")—Devonport is the largest operational naval base in the UK and Western Europe.[[195]](#cite_note-199) Each base hosts a flotilla command under a [commodore](/wiki/Commodore_(RN) "Commodore (RN)"), responsible for the provision of operational capability using the ships and submarines within the flotilla. UK Commando Force is similarly commanded by a [brigadier](/wiki/Brigadier "Brigadier") and based in Plymouth.[[196]](#cite_note-200)

The Royal Navy has historically maintained [Royal Navy Dockyards](/wiki/Royal_Navy_Dockyards "Royal Navy Dockyards") around the world.[[197]](#cite_note-201) [Dockyards](/wiki/Dockyard "Dockyard") of the Royal Navy are harbours where ships are overhauled and refitted. Only four are operating today; at Devonport, [Faslane](/wiki/Faslane_Naval_Base "Faslane Naval Base"), [Rosyth](/wiki/Rosyth_Dockyard "Rosyth Dockyard") and at Portsmouth.[[198]](#cite_note-UKNDABlackham-202) A Naval Base Review was undertaken in 2006 and early 2007, the outcome being announced by Secretary of State for Defence, [Des Browne](/wiki/Des_Browne "Des Browne"), confirming that all would remain however some reductions in manpower were anticipated.[[199]](#cite_note-203)

The academy where initial training for future Royal Navy officers takes place is Britannia Royal Naval College, located on a hill overlooking Dartmouth, Devon. Basic training for future [ratings](/wiki/Naval_rating "Naval rating") takes place at HMS *Raleigh* at [Torpoint](/wiki/Torpoint "Torpoint"), [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), close to HMNB Devonport.[[200]](#cite_note-204)

Significant numbers of naval personnel are employed within the Ministry of Defence, Defence Equipment and Support and on exchange with the Army and Royal Air Force. Small numbers are also on exchange within other government departments and with allied fleets, such as the United States Navy. The navy also posts personnel in small units around the world to support ongoing operations and maintain standing commitments. Nineteen personnel are stationed in Gibraltar to support the small [Gibraltar Squadron](/wiki/Gibraltar_Squadron "Gibraltar Squadron"), the RN's only permanent overseas squadron. Some personnel are also based at [East Cove Military Port](/wiki/East_Cove_Military_Port "East Cove Military Port") and [RAF Mount Pleasant](/wiki/RAF_Mount_Pleasant "RAF Mount Pleasant") in the Falkland Islands to support APT(S). Small numbers of personnel are based in [Diego Garcia](/wiki/Diego_Garcia "Diego Garcia") (Naval Party 1002), Miami (NP 1011 – [AUTEC](/wiki/AUTEC "AUTEC")), Singapore (NP 1022), [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai") (NP 1023) and elsewhere.[[201]](#cite_note-205)

On 6 December 2014, the [Foreign and Commonwealth Office](/wiki/Foreign_and_Commonwealth_Office "Foreign and Commonwealth Office") announced it would expand the UK's naval facilities in [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain") to support larger Royal Navy ships deployed to the Persian Gulf. Once completed, it became the UK's first permanent military base located [East of Suez](/wiki/East_of_Suez "East of Suez") since it withdrew from the region in 1971. The base is reportedly large enough to accommodate Type 45 destroyers and *Queen Elizabeth*-class aircraft carriers.[[202]](#cite_note-206)[[203]](#cite_note-207)[[204]](#cite_note-208)

#### Bases in the United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Bases in the United Kingdom")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/HMS_Vigilant_alongside_Faslane_Naval_Base._MOD_45147682.jpg/250px-HMS_Vigilant_alongside_Faslane_Naval_Base._MOD_45147682.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Vigilant_alongside_Faslane_Naval_Base._MOD_45147682.jpg)

[HMS *Vigilant*](/wiki/HMS_Vigilant_(S30) "HMS Vigilant (S30)") alongside [Faslane Naval Base](/wiki/HMNB_Clyde "HMNB Clyde")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Royal_Navy_Commando_Helicopter_Force_Merlin_HC3-3A%2C_British_Army_Wildcat_AH1_%2828418553256%29.jpg/250px-Royal_Navy_Commando_Helicopter_Force_Merlin_HC3-3A%2C_British_Army_Wildcat_AH1_%2828418553256%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Navy_Commando_Helicopter_Force_Merlin_HC3-3A,_British_Army_Wildcat_AH1_(28418553256).jpg)

A [Merlin HC3](/wiki/AgustaWestland_AW101 "AgustaWestland AW101") and [Wildcat AH1](/wiki/AgustaWestland_AW159_Wildcat "AgustaWestland AW159 Wildcat"), both part of the Commando Helicopter Force, at [RNAS Yeovilton](/wiki/RNAS_Yeovilton_(HMS_Heron) "RNAS Yeovilton (HMS Heron)")

* [HMNB Devonport](/wiki/HMNB_Devonport "HMNB Devonport") (HMS *Drake*) – This is currently the largest operational naval base in Western Europe. [Devonport](/wiki/Devonport,_Plymouth "Devonport, Plymouth")'s flotilla consists of most of the [Type 23 frigates](/wiki/Type_23_frigate "Type 23 frigate"). In the past, Devonport was also home to some of the RN's submarine service.[[205]](#cite_note-209)
* [HMNB Portsmouth](/wiki/HMNB_Portsmouth "HMNB Portsmouth") (HMS *Nelson*) – This is home to the Queen Elizabeth Class supercarriers. [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth") is also the home to the Type 45 Daring Class Destroyer and a moderate fleet of Type 23 frigates as well as [Overseas Patrol Squadron](/wiki/Overseas_Patrol_Squadron "Overseas Patrol Squadron").[[206]](#cite_note-210)
* [HMNB Clyde](/wiki/HMNB_Clyde "HMNB Clyde") (HMS *Neptune*) – This is situated in Central Scotland along the [River Clyde](/wiki/River_Clyde "River Clyde"). Faslane is known as the home of the UK's nuclear deterrent, as it maintains the fleet of *Vanguard*-class ballistic missile (SSBN) submarines, as well as the fleet of *Astute*-class fleet (SSN) submarines. Faslane will become the home to all Royal Navy submarines, and thus the RN Submarine Service. As a result, 43 Commando (Fleet Protection Group) are stationed in Faslane alongside to guard the base as well as The Royal Naval Armaments Depot at Coulport. The newly established Mine and Threat Exploitation Group (MTXG) is also based within Faslane as a successor to the Sandown class mine hunters. Moreover, Faslane is also home to Faslane Patrol Boat Squadron (FPBS) who operates a fleet of Archer class patrol vessels.[[207]](#cite_note-211)[[208]](#cite_note-212)
* [RNAS Yeovilton](/wiki/RNAS_Yeovilton "RNAS Yeovilton") (HMS *Heron*) – Yeovilton is home to Commando Helicopter Force and Wildcat Maritime Force.[[209]](#cite_note-213)
* [RNAS Culdrose](/wiki/RNAS_Culdrose "RNAS Culdrose") (HMS *Seahawk*) – This is home to Mk2 Merlins, primarily tasked with conducting Anti-Submarine Warfare (ASW) and Airborne Early Warning (AEW). [Culdrose](/wiki/RNAS_Culdrose_(HMS_Seahawk) "RNAS Culdrose (HMS Seahawk)") is also currently the largest helicopter base in Europe.[[210]](#cite_note-214)
* [HMS *Gannet*](/wiki/HMS_Gannet_(1971_shore_establishment) "HMS Gannet (1971 shore establishment)") – Previously known as RNAS Prestwick. Previously used for Defence of the Clyde and Search and Rescue tasking, it is now used primarily as a FOB for ASW Merlins deployed from RNAS Culdrose to support the SSBN and defence of the Clyde tasking.[[211]](#cite_note-215)

#### Bases abroad

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Bases abroad")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/ZH850_-_WM-462_EHI_EH-101_Merlin_HM2_%28Mk111%29_%28cn_50125-RN30%29_Royal_Navy._%2810475633143%29.jpg/250px-ZH850_-_WM-462_EHI_EH-101_Merlin_HM2_%28Mk111%29_%28cn_50125-RN30%29_Royal_Navy._%2810475633143%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:ZH850_-_WM-462_EHI_EH-101_Merlin_HM2_(Mk111)_(cn_50125-RN30)_Royal_Navy._(10475633143).jpg)

A Royal Navy [Merlin HM2](/wiki/AgustaWestland_AW101 "AgustaWestland AW101") at [RNAS Culdrose](/wiki/RNAS_Culdrose_(HMS_Seahawk) "RNAS Culdrose (HMS Seahawk)")

* [UK National Support Element (Bahrain)](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Naval_Support_Facility "United Kingdom Naval Support Facility") – Previously the home port for vessels deployed on [Operation Kipion](/wiki/Operation_Kipion "Operation Kipion") and acts as the hub of the Royal Navy's operations in the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf"), [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea") and [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean").[[212]](#cite_note-216)
* [UK Joint Logistics Support Base](/wiki/UK_Joint_Logistics_Support_Base "UK Joint Logistics Support Base") ([Oman](/wiki/Oman "Oman")) – A logistical support facility which is strategically located in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") but outside the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf").[[213]](#cite_note-UKJLSB-217)
* [British Defence Singapore Support Unit](/wiki/British_Defence_Singapore_Support_Unit "British Defence Singapore Support Unit") ([Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore")) – A remnant of [HMNB Singapore](/wiki/HMNB_Singapore "HMNB Singapore") which repairs and resupplies Royal Navy ships in the [Asia Pacific](/wiki/Asia_Pacific "Asia Pacific").[[214]](#cite_note-BDSSU-218) It is the primary logistics support hub for the Royal Navy offshore patrol vessels assigned to the Asia-Pacific region, [HMS *Tamar*](/wiki/HMS_Tamar_(P233) "HMS Tamar (P233)") and [HMS *Spey*](/wiki/HMS_Spey_(P234) "HMS Spey (P234)").[[215]](#cite_note-219)
* [HMNB Gibraltar](/wiki/British_Forces_Gibraltar#HM_Dockyard,_Gibraltar "British Forces Gibraltar") – A current Royal Navy dockyard in [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") which is still used for docking, repairs, training and resupply.[[216]](#cite_note-British_Forces_Gibraltar-220) Vessels permanently based with the [Gibraltar Squadron](/wiki/Gibraltar_Squadron "Gibraltar Squadron") include the [*Cutlass*-class fast patrol boats](/wiki/Cutlass-class_patrol_vessel "Cutlass-class patrol vessel"), HMS *Cutlass* and HMS *Dagger*.[[217]](#cite_note-Gibdock2-221)
* [Mare Harbour](/wiki/Mare_Harbour "Mare Harbour") ([Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands")) – Serves as the port facility for [RAF Mount Pleasant](/wiki/RAF_Mount_Pleasant "RAF Mount Pleasant"), the main British base in the Falkland Islands. Mare Harbour incorporates several berths which support Royal Navy and marine services vessels operating in the South Atlantic. The facility also supports the [British Antarctic Survey](/wiki/British_Antarctic_Survey "British Antarctic Survey") ship, [RRS Sir David Attenborough](/wiki/RRS_Sir_David_Attenborough "RRS Sir David Attenborough"), when she operates in Antarctic waters during the regional summer.[[218]](#cite_note-222)

## Titles and naming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Titles and naming")]

### Of the Navy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Of the Navy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/HMS_Richmond_MOD_45155880.jpg/250px-HMS_Richmond_MOD_45155880.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Richmond_MOD_45155880.jpg)

[Type 23 frigates](/wiki/Type_23_frigate "Type 23 frigate"), also known as "Duke class", are named after [British dukes](/wiki/Duke#United_Kingdom "Duke").

The navy was referred to as the "Navy Royal" at the time of its founding in 1546, and this title remained in use into the Stuart period. During the interregnum, the commonwealth under [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") replaced many historical names and titles, with the fleet then referred to as the "Commonwealth Navy". The navy was renamed once again after the restoration in 1660 to the present title.[[219]](#cite_note-223)

Today, the navy of the United Kingdom is commonly referred to as the "Royal Navy" both in the United Kingdom and other countries. Navies of other [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") countries [where the British monarch is also head of state](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") include their national name, e.g. Royal Australian Navy. Some navies of other monarchies, such as the *Koninklijke Marine* ([Royal Netherlands Navy](/wiki/Royal_Netherlands_Navy "Royal Netherlands Navy")) and *Kungliga Flottan* ([Royal Swedish Navy](/wiki/Royal_Swedish_Navy "Royal Swedish Navy")), are also called "Royal Navy" in their own language. The Danish Navy uses the term "Royal" incorporated in its official name ([Royal Danish Navy](/wiki/Royal_Danish_Navy "Royal Danish Navy")), but only "Flåden" (Navy) in everyday speech.[[220]](#cite_note-224) The French Navy, despite France being a republic since 1870, is often nicknamed *"La Royale"* (literally: The Royal).[[221]](#cite_note-225)

### Of ships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Of ships")]

Main article: [List of ships of the Royal Navy](/wiki/List_of_ships_of_the_Royal_Navy "List of ships of the Royal Navy")

See also: [List of active Royal Navy ships](/wiki/List_of_active_Royal_Navy_ships "List of active Royal Navy ships"), [Naming conventions for destroyers of the Royal Navy](/wiki/Naming_conventions_for_destroyers_of_the_Royal_Navy "Naming conventions for destroyers of the Royal Navy"), and [Type system of the Royal Navy](/wiki/Type_system_of_the_Royal_Navy "Type system of the Royal Navy")

Royal Navy ships in commission are prefixed since 1789 with His Majesty's Ship (or "Her Majesty's Ship", when the monarch is a queen), abbreviated to "HMS"; for example, [HMS *Beagle*](/wiki/HMS_Beagle "HMS Beagle"). Submarines are styled HM Submarine, also abbreviated "HMS". Names are allocated to ships and submarines by a naming committee within the MOD[[222]](#cite_note-226) and given by class, with the names of ships within a class often being thematic (for example, the Type 23s are named after [British dukes](/wiki/Duke#United_Kingdom "Duke")) or traditional (for example, the [*Invincible*-class aircraft carriers](/wiki/Invincible-class_aircraft_carrier "Invincible-class aircraft carrier") all carry the names of famous historic ships). The monarch formally approves all ship names, with [King George V](/wiki/King_George_V "King George V") vetoing naming a ship after [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") in 1911.[[223]](#cite_note-227) Names are frequently re-used, offering a new ship the rich heritage, battle honours and traditions of her predecessors. Often, a particular vessel class will be named after the first ship of that type to be built. As well as a name, each ship and submarine of the Royal Navy and the Royal Fleet Auxiliary is given a [pennant number](/wiki/Pennant_number "Pennant number") which in part denotes its role. For example, the destroyer [HMS *Daring* (D32)](/wiki/HMS_Daring_(D32) "HMS Daring (D32)") displays the pennant number 'D32'.[[224]](#cite_note-228)

## Ranks, rates and insignia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Ranks, rates and insignia")]

The Royal Navy ranks, rates and insignia form part of the uniform of the Royal Navy. The Royal Navy uniform is the pattern on which many of the uniforms of the other national navies of the world are based (e.g. [Ranks and insignia of NATO navies officers](/wiki/Ranks_and_insignia_of_NATO_navies_officers "Ranks and insignia of NATO navies officers"), [Uniforms of the United States Navy](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_United_States_Navy "Uniforms of the United States Navy"), [Uniforms of the Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_Royal_Canadian_Navy "Uniforms of the Royal Canadian Navy"), [French Naval Uniforms](/wiki/French_Navy#Uniforms "French Navy")).[[225]](#cite_note-229)

## Customs and traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Customs and traditions")]

Main article: [Customs and traditions of the Royal Navy](/wiki/Customs_and_traditions_of_the_Royal_Navy "Customs and traditions of the Royal Navy")

### Traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Traditions")]

The Royal Navy has several formal customs and traditions including the use of ensigns and ships badges. Royal Navy ships have several ensigns used when under way and when in port. Commissioned ships and submarines wear the [White Ensign](/wiki/White_Ensign "White Ensign") at the stern whilst alongside during daylight hours and at the main-mast whilst under way. When alongside, the *Union Jack* is flown from the [jackstaff](/wiki/Jackstaff "Jackstaff") at the bow, and can only be flown under way either to signal a court-martial is in progress or to indicate the presence of an admiral of the fleet on-board (including the Lord High Admiral or the monarch).[[230]](#cite_note-235)

The [Fleet Review](/wiki/Fleet_Review,_Royal_Navy "Fleet Review, Royal Navy") is an irregular tradition of assembling the fleet before the monarch. The first review on record was held in 1400, and the most recent review as of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit) was held on 28 June 2005 to mark the bi-centenary of the Battle of Trafalgar; 167 ships from many different nations attended with the Royal Navy supplying 67.[[231]](#cite_note-236)

### "Jackspeak"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: \"Jackspeak\"")]

There are several less formal traditions including service nicknames and Naval slang, known as *"Jackspeak"*.[[232]](#cite_note-237) The nicknames include "The Andrew" (of uncertain origin, possibly after a zealous [press ganger](/wiki/Impressment "Impressment"))[[233]](#cite_note-238)[[234]](#cite_note-239) and "The Senior Service".[[235]](#cite_note-jackspeak-240)[[236]](#cite_note-241) British sailors are referred to as "Jack" (or "Jenny"), or more widely as "Matelots". Royal Marines are fondly known as "Bootnecks" or often just as "Royals". A compendium of Naval slang was brought together by Commander [A.T.L. Covey-Crump](/wiki/A.T.L._Covey-Crump "A.T.L. Covey-Crump") and his name has in itself become the subject of Naval slang; *Covey-Crump*.[[235]](#cite_note-jackspeak-240) A game traditionally played by the Navy is the four-player board game known as "[Uckers](/wiki/Uckers "Uckers")". This is similar to [Ludo](/wiki/Ludo_(board_game) "Ludo (board game)") and it is regarded as easy to learn, but difficult to play well.[[237]](#cite_note-242)

## Navy cadets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Navy cadets")]

The Royal Navy sponsors or supports three youth organisations:

* [Volunteer Cadet Corps](/wiki/Volunteer_Cadet_Corps "Volunteer Cadet Corps") – consisting of Royal Naval Volunteer Cadet Corps and Royal Marines Volunteer Cadet Corps, the VCC was the first youth organisation officially supported or sponsored by the Admiralty in 1901.[[238]](#cite_note-243)
* [Combined Cadet Force](/wiki/Combined_Cadet_Force "Combined Cadet Force") – in schools, specifically the Royal Navy Section and the Royal Marines Section.[[239]](#cite_note-244)
* [Sea Cadets](/wiki/Sea_Cadet_Corps_(United_Kingdom) "Sea Cadet Corps (United Kingdom)") – supporting teenagers who are interested in naval matters, consisting of the Sea Cadets and the [Royal Marines Cadets](/wiki/Royal_Marines_Cadets "Royal Marines Cadets").[[240]](#cite_note-245)

The above organisations are the responsibility of the CUY branch of Commander Core Training and Recruiting (COMCORE) who reports to [Flag Officer Sea Training](/wiki/Flag_Officer_Sea_Training_(United_Kingdom) "Flag Officer Sea Training (United Kingdom)") (FOST).[[241]](#cite_note-246)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Navy&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

See also: [Nautical fiction](/wiki/Nautical_fiction "Nautical fiction")

The Royal Navy of the 18th century is depicted in many [novels](/wiki/Mutiny_on_the_Bounty_(novel) "Mutiny on the Bounty (novel)") and several films dramatising the voyage and [mutiny on the *Bounty*](/wiki/Mutiny_on_the_Bounty "Mutiny on the Bounty").[[242]](#cite_note-247) The Royal Navy's Napoleonic campaigns of the early 19th century are also a popular subject of historical novels. Some of the best-known are [Patrick O'Brian](/wiki/Patrick_O%27Brian "Patrick O'Brian")'s [Aubrey-Maturin series](/wiki/Aubrey-Maturin_series "Aubrey-Maturin series")[[243]](#cite_note-248) and [C. S. Forester](/wiki/C._S._Forester "C. S. Forester")'s [Horatio Hornblower](/wiki/Horatio_Hornblower "Horatio Hornblower") chronicles.[[244]](#cite_note-249)

The Navy can also be seen in numerous films and television programmes.[[245]](#cite_note-250)[[246]](#cite_note-251)[[247]](#cite_note-252)[[248]](#cite_note-253)[[249]](#cite_note-254)[[250]](#cite_note-255)[[251]](#cite_note-256) Television documentaries about the Royal Navy include: *Empire of the Seas: How the Navy Forged the Modern World*, a four-part documentary depicting Britain's rise as a naval superpower, up until the First World War;[[252]](#cite_note-257) *[Sailor](/wiki/Sailor_(TV_series) "Sailor (TV series)")*, about life on the aircraft carrier [HMS *Ark Royal*](/wiki/HMS_Ark_Royal_(R09) "HMS Ark Royal (R09)");[[253]](#cite_note-258) and *Submarine*, about the [submarine captains' training course](/wiki/Submarine_Command_Course "Submarine Command Course"), 'The Perisher'.[[254]](#cite_note-259) There have also been [Channel 5](/wiki/Channel_5_(British_TV_channel) "Channel 5 (British TV channel)") documentaries such as *Royal Navy Submarine Mission*, following a nuclear-powered fleet submarine.[[255]](#cite_note-260)
